strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce are wearing the required safety gear or personal protective equipment at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public; 

closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP and standards by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes; and

formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the appropriate sanctions or penalties to be applied to contractors found in violation of the CSHP to ensure accountability and adherence to safety protocols.

observations.recommendation:

The members of the Inventory Committee conduct the annual physical inventory, prepare the RPCPPE and/or may seek assistance or coordinate with the department heads to make inventory of municipal properties in their respective offices, so as to address the manpower constraint in the conduct of physical count.

The Property Officer/GSO and Municipal Accountant update the property/supply ledger cards and subsidiary ledgers, respectively, reconcile the same, and make the necessary adjustments.  Additional staff to address the concern on manpower constraints may also be considered.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that Management require the Municipal Assessor to expedite the titling of recorded parcels of land in compliance with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 and Section 39 of PD No. 1445 to establish ownership thereof and to fully protect the interest of the municipality.  


We have further recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Assessor to exercise added endeavor to locate documents and determine the details of unidentified land accounts for proper recognition and establishment of ownership thereon.

29 parcels of land identified by the Municipal Assessor as property of the LGU were not recorded in the books and eight parcels of land recorded at market value.

We recommended that the Management:

Enjoin the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the General Service Officer and Municipal Assessor to exert further efforts in retrieval of documents required to establish ownership and cost valuation of the unrecorded land to facilitate its recording in the books, at acquisition cost, or fair value, if acquired through non-exchange transaction;

Make the necessary adjustment by recording the land account at acquisition cost, or fair value, if acquired through non-exchange transaction, based on available supporting documents; and


Initiate the transfer of ownership of road lots to various barangays which are not held for use by the Municipal Government.
observations.managementAction:
Ownership on the ten parcels of land was documented through tax declarations instead of Certificates of Title.  Additionally, three of five parcels of land have been transferred to the Municipality since 2022.  

The Municipal Accountant started to retrieve documents/ records but not yet completely found.





The Municipal Accountant closely coordinated with the Municipal Assessor.

prepare the RCPPE with complete details such as the number of units actually on hand, condition whether serviceable or not and whereabouts of the property;

Prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action on non- existing/ missing properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CAD and GSD to reconcile their records and exert earnest efforts to locate previous records pertaining to the building, school building and PPE which are not fully depreciated to date for proper disposition.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GSD to undertake the necessary procedures for the immediate disposal of the unserviceable and/or obsolete PPE amounting to P21,915,908.15 to avoid its further deterioration and depreciation in value.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CENRO to coordinate with the DENR-EMB for the disposal or return of the damaged equipment.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GSD to:
Coordinate with the repair shop to determine the condition of the eight service vehicles, the reasons surrounding the inability to deliver the services/repairs required by the City and/or initiate the immediate retrieval of the vehicles to avoid unnecessary storage fees; and
Provide explanation as to the reasons why the eight service vehicles were left unattended in the repair shop.

We recommended that SJMC Medical Director:
Implement a real-time tracking system for claim submission and status monitoring;
Ensure that RTH claims meet all the PhilHealth documentation requirements before its refiling; and
Follow-up the status of the In Process/pending claims for reimbursement, particularly those that have been submitted in the previous years.

We recommended that the CENRO and CDRRMO conduct a thorough planning and review of the annual LCCAP and come up with a reasonable estimate for each PPA to avoid excessive fund allocation and repetitive programs and projects which are unimplemented year after year, to ensure the full achievement of the City’s mandate on climate change.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters and ensure that the same is received by the concerned persons and seek legal remedies so that the past due receivables will be settled the soonest possible time. For the receivables from deceased individuals, we recommend that the LCE seek legal remedies for the institution of claim against their estates, hence, would have additional funds to finance projects for the benefit the constituents.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the LDRRMO-designate, MAO, MSWDO, and MHO to submit immediately to the Municipal Accountant the duly accomplished/certified distribution list of the issued/distributed welfare goods and food supplies amounting to P3,737,987.29, farm implements and equipment aggregating P227,179,958.00, construction materials amounting to P7,047,235.00, and drugs and medicines amounting to  P3,459,455.00  to the end-users respectively,  and as his basis in recording the items under the expense account.
Also, require the accountant to recognize the issuance of inventories aggregating P5,929,687.00, thus the affected accounts will be correctly stated in the FS.
Henceforth, the Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the GSO to submit the SSMI, supported  with a duly accomplished RIS for all issued/distributed inventory including the issuance of office supplies, accountable forms, plates and stickers, food supplies and other supplies aggregating P4,384,414.22, and to regularly prepare and furnish these forms to the Municipal Accountant together with the duly accomplished and certified distribution list of all goods distributed to the end-users as his basis in the preparation of journal entry to record the expenditures using appropriate expenditure accounts, hence to present fairly the affected accounts in the FS.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to locate or trace the documents of recorded infrastructure assets and make the necessary adjustments in the books to reflect the correct book value of the foregoing infrastructure assets, hence to present fairly and reliably the FS.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a correcting/adjusting journal entry to restate the financial statements of the ensuing year, hence reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts in the FS, to wit:
GF Books:
SEF Books:
Henceforth, properly record the succeeding transactions in their correct account classification hence, to present fairly and accurately the financial position and performance of the LGU, thus could be relied upon in decision making.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to make a compilation of any record/documentation and prepare the corresponding assessment/evaluation report as regards the long outstanding liabilities to support the possible reversion in order not to overstate the liability accounts.  Likewise, attach Subsidiary Ledgers and all available records to support the amount of the JEV and attach a copy in case of reversion to the unappropriated surplus.  If there may be claims in the future, the JEV would serve as the basis in requesting for a supplemental budget for the settlement of the payable.
The Audit Team also recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to make a compilation of any record/documentation and prepare the corresponding assessment/evaluation report as regards the long outstanding payable amounting to P104,579.43 to support the possible reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the GF in order not to overstate the liability accounts.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the variance of P699,469.28 with the records of the Provincial Accountant to ensure correct and accurate balance of the Due to LGUs account in the FS and to ensure the correctness of the remittances made, thus FS will be fairly presented.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the supporting documents and explanations of the transactions recorded in the GJ for CY 2024 and henceforth, to continually support the GJ with the same documentation to ascertain the validity and propriety of the financial transactions recorded therein and the correctness of journal entries made.
Further, the Audit Team recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to follow the prescribed procedures in the recording of financial transactions from the JEV to the corresponding journals and then to the GL as embodied in Annexes 1 to 5 of Volume II of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, ensuring that the necessary details, explanations, computations and supporting evidence/s are incorporated therein, hence to facilitate systematic verification of accounts presented in the FS.
Moreover, provide the necessary details in the Schedule of Receivables and Payables such as the names and addresses of the debtors and creditors, the specific description or nature of each account, and the date of accruals or incurrence of the receivables and obligations to facilitate verification of the same.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE oblige the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to cause the immediate submission of the DVs for audit and verification purposes, hence to ascertain the validity and propriety of the transactions and proper audit action, hence, any deficiencies will be communicated to management for the latter’s immediate appropriate action. Further, Management strictly observes the timely submission of transaction documents to facilitate the review thereon and avoid further audit action as provided in Section 39 (3) and 122 (2) of PD No. 1445.

The Audit Team recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of Section 44 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs on the correct procedures of check disbursement process, ensuring to secure first the certification on the availability of appropriation before processing the transaction for payment, hence, to determine that the disbursement has a valid appropriation and to avoid the incurrence of appropriations overdraft.  Likewise, ensure that the forms involved are completely dated to ascertain that all transactions went through the correct process of disbursement, thus, ascertaining the validity, and propriety of the financial transactions.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Assessor to oversee the immediate transfer of the TDs of the 10 parcels of land aggregating P2,723,501.62 in the name of the Municipality of San Juan and to submit immediately the certified true copies of the TCT of the 44 parcels of land aggregating P113,622,471.06 and TDs of the 10 parcels of land aggregating P48,415,900.00, to ascertain absolute ownership over these parcels of land and to avoid possible ownership dispute which can result to wastage of government funds.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents to the Municipal Accountant who will verify the completeness and accuracy thereof and subsequently submit the same to the Auditor in order for the latter to verify the validity, propriety and correctness of these documents and compliance of said transactions with the abovementioned provisions of law and regulations; and
Henceforth, require the Municipal Accountant and concerned officials of strict compliance with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 by supporting all disbursement vouchers with complete and accurate documents before effecting payment thereof to ascertain the validity, propriety and correctness of the transactions.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the submission of the liquidation reports and supporting documents of the aforementioned cash advances to ascertain the validity, propriety and correctness of these transactions.
Likewise, the Audit Team recommended that concerned officials and employees strictly comply with the provisions set forth in Items 4 and 5 of COA Circular 97-002 in the succeeding cash advances, and direct the Municipal Accountant to monitor its compliance, hence to facilitate efficient control over the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
Instruct the GSO to prepare Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and require the drivers to prepare Report of Official Travels per vehicle and properly accomplish the Driver’s Trip Tickets pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 in order to provide adequate basis in determining the reasonableness of fuel consumption.
Also, require the supplier to immediately refund the excess payment of gasoline expenses amounting to P5,000.00, or instruct the Municipal Accountant to deduct the amount from payments of succeeding purchases due to the supplier, hence to restitute the overpaid amount.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the GSO to assign property/inventory number of all properties and inventories to facilitate identification of properties which is useful in the conduct of annual physical inventory. Likewise, this will facilitate proper security and custodial of the properties of the Municipality.
Henceforth, the Audit Team recommended that the property/inventory numbers of motor vehicles shall be indicated in the driver’s trip ticket for easy verification of the validity and reasonableness of issued fuel. Likewise, the property/inventory numbers of properties for repair and maintenance shall be provided in the Repair History Report and Pre-Inspection Report to enable verification if properties under repair are duly owned by the Municipality. Also, this would facilitate proactive scheduling for maintenance of properties which will reduce downtime and costs, hence resulting in the efficient usage of government resources.

The Audit Team recommended that henceforth, Management strictly comply with the provisions set forth in Section 68 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 and Section 5 Volume 2 of the Government Procurement Manual in the assessment of the number of CD delays in the performance of the contract and the computation of liquidated damages which shall be at least equal to one-tenth of one percent (0.001) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every day of delay to ensure timely completion of the contract for the immediate usage and enjoyment by the intended beneficiaries/constituents of the LGU.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare the disbursement vouchers for the remittance of the shares/dues to the barangays in the total amount of P2,380,547.73; and
henceforth, require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to release the share of each barangay on RPT and CTCs on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter to enable the latter to utilize the funds for its operations.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer to abide strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 by:
properly accomplishing and updating the signboards/billboards by providing all the relevant information pertaining to the project at hand to enable the public to see and read relevant information for them to assess if PPAs are fairly implemented and justifiably completed which in turn promotes good governance through transparency; and
informing the Audit Team within ten days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted for verification.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
ensure that identified programs/projects under the 20% DF are implemented as planned to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the municipality;
require the concerned officials to review the feasibility of the unimplemented programs/projects and plan for the necessary realignment of these funds to a more viable priority development project; and
encourage the LGU to formulate plans that are attainable within a year to maximize the benefits that could be derived from the full utilization of the fund.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE:
instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Engineer to ensure the optimal utilization of the RA 7171 and 8240 Municipal Share through proper and timely implementation of the identified PPAs so that the benefits expected from its timely completion will be delivered to the tobacco farmers.
require the concerned officials to review the feasibility of the unimplemented projects and plan for the necessary realignment of these funds to a more viable development projects that help in the attainment of the purpose and objective of the fund which is to advance the self-reliance of the farmer beneficiaries.
propose to the Sanggunian the re-appropriation of the prior year’s savings from implemented projects thus, maximize implementation and utilization of the funds for the benefit of the tobacco farmers.

The Audit Team recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to revert to the unappropriated surplus of the GF the unspent balance of LDRRMF for the year 2018 amlounting to P537,605.64 and every year thereafter to have additional funds for other social services of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:
Exhaust all possible means to collect the long outstanding receivables, particularly those with available supporting documents, and ensure that all collection efforts, such as the issuance of demand letters, are properly documented;
Intensify efforts to locate all relevant documents related to the long outstanding receivables to establish their existence and validity, and the accountability of concerned parties; and
Submit a request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts for the receivables that are confirmed to be uncollectible but are supported with the required documents, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to recognize an allowance for impairment of receivables in accordance with IPSAS to ensure the fair presentation of the Municipality’s financial statements.
We further recommended that the Honorable Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer to assess the current manpower complement of the Accounting Office and implement augmentation measures, if necessary, to ensure adequate staffing and improved operational efficiency.
Management has already conducted a thorough search for documentation related to the long-outstanding receivables. They remain committed to implementing the audit recommendations and will formally document all actions taken to support their request for write-off of these dormant receivables.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and MBO to:
Recognize and record obligations based on the total contract amounts upon contract award, including retention fees and future liabilities; and
Maintain and reconcile monthly the RAAOs and SAAOB.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to restate the CY 2024 SCBAA and revise the Reconciliation between Actual Amounts on a Comparable Basis as presented in the SCBAA and Statement of Financial Performance (SFPer), incorporating therein the noted discrepancies.
Management conveyed compliance with the audit recommendations.

To strengthen internal controls over inventory management, enhance accountability over government property, and ensure the fair presentation of financial information, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
Reverse the previously recorded adjusting journal entry that reclassified the semi-expendable items from OSMI to Semi-Expendable Expenses in the current year;
Appropriately restate the financial statements of prior years to reflect the recognition of the related expenses in the period when the items were actually issued or incurred, in line with the accrual basis of accounting as mandated by prevailing government accounting standards; and
Ensure strict compliance with the principles of accrual accounting, whereby expenses are recognized in the period they are incurred, regardless of when payment or documentation occurs.
Designated Property Custodian to:
Conduct regular physical counts of inventories in accordance with the provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs, without undue reliance on or waiting for the participation of the Accounting Unit, as their functions are operationally distinct and independent; and
Prepare the RPCI on a semi-annual basis and ensure its timely submission to the Audit Team, with a copy furnished to the Accounting Unit for purposes of reconciliation and accurate financial recording.
The Management committed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of charging AICS and regular expenses to the PCF;
Concerned and appropriate municipal officials to formally adopt a written policy on the handling of the PCF through an Ordinance taking into account the policies and guidelines under the Circular; and
Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, and the MBO to exercise diligence and prudence when processing payments charged to the PCF.
Management expressed its commitment to implement the audit recommendations. A new SDO will be designated to manage cash advances solely for the AICS program.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to conduct competitive bidding for the procurement of meals and snacks by entering into a FA with the winning bidder to streamline and simplify the procurement process.
Management signified their commitment to implement the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MENRO to:
Temporarily equip the existing dump trucks with a secure covering, such as net made from available materials, to reduce spillage and associated health risks during transport; and
Update the markings on the garbage trucks to include the contact number of the Municipality to facilitate public communication and comply with the requirements of RA No. 9003.
Management informed the Audit Team that the garbage trucks are currently being utilized for multiple operational purposes. Nonetheless, they signified their compliance with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Coordinate with the Municipal Agriculturist regarding the supporting documents for the return of the ₱512,270.95 unutilized balance to DA; and
Establish and maintain individual subsidiary ledgers for all fund transfers from NGAS to ensure proper monitoring and timely liquidation of unutilized balances.
Management signified their commitment to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the:

a. GSO to prepare the RPCPPE with the quantity per count column of the PPE in
accordance with the format as stated in the above-cited regulation of the Manual,
and maintain a complete Property Card for all PPEs of the agency;
b. GSO to conduct physical count for CIP accounts amounting to P10,037,564.40
and include the same in the RPCPPE; and
c. The Inventory Committee, the Municipal Accountant and GSO to conduct the one-time
cleansing of account following the guidelines and procedures under COA Circular No.
2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and submit to the Audit Team the proposed timeline
for the immediate completion thereof. Henceforth, ensure the submission of RPCPPE
within the date prescribed by the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Review the noted observations regarding depreciation and effect the necessary
adjustments. Submit copies of the adjusting journal entries made to the audit
team for further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly observe the policies on the
recognition of depreciation for PPE prescribed in PAG 3 and 6 of IPSAS 17;
b. Ensure that the subsequent cash advances will be recorded in its proper account;
c. Record the withheld taxes to BIR under the Due to BIR account and ensure that all
withholdings in the ensuing years are properly recorded in their appropriate
accounts; and
d. Record the advance collections during the year and in the succeeding years under
the Deferred Real Property Tax and Deferred Special Education Tax accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer, Municipal
Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to:

a. Cause the immediate implementation of the remaining unimplemented project and
the completion of the on-going projects identified in the audit finding and provide an
update as to the status of those projects. If the projects will no longer be
implemented and the funds are reprogrammed to another projects, include those
along with the reasons in the status for submission to the audit team; and
b. Consider the unexpended balances of the completed projects as of December 31,
2024 of P809,186.36 for reversion to unappropriated surplus for possible allocation
to other developmental projects.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:































































































































































































































The recommended text is:





Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) to
implement the programs, projects and activities under Current, Continuing
Appropriation and Trust Liability in the succeeding years;
Municipal Planning and Development Officer in coordination with the MDRRMO to
carefully plan the PPAs that will be charged under the Trust Liability  DRRMF
account under the Trust Fund and incorporate such activities in the LDRRMFIP;
and
Municipal Budget Officer in coordination with the MDRRMO appropriate and
obligate allowable expenses to the LDRRMF as identified in the above regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to
accountable officers without liquidating first their previous cash advances and
with the necessary certification attach in the DV that previous cash advances had
been liquidated;
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that cash advances granted do not exceed the
maximum cash accountabilities, otherwise, require the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to consider increasing the fidelity bond of the Special
Disbursing Officers to sufficiently cover the total daily accountabilities in
accordance with the schedule provided under Treasury Circular No. 02-2019; and
c. Accountable Officers to liquidate the cash advances within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure that the project
concreting of farm to market road at Barangay Sapangan (Phase II)is immediately
implemented and ensure that the succeeding proposed projects are readily
implementable within the prescribed timelines and properly plan the same so
issues related to the site/land title are resolved before the implementation of the
project as prescribed in Section 4.2.4 of the DBM LBC Nos. 151 and 154; and
b. Municipal Local Finance Committee to post the quarterly reports on fund
utilization and status of program/project implementation on the LGU’s website
and send written notices to Secretary of DBM, Secretary of DILG, Chairperson of
the NTF-ELCAC, coursed through the NTF-ELCAC Vice Chairperson and RTF-
ELCAC concerned. Speaker of the House of Representatives, President of the
Senate of the Philippines, and the Chairpersons of the House Committee on
Appropriations and Senate Committee on Finance. Ensure the preparation of the
quarterly reports when there are receipt of funds for the implementation of
programs and projects chargeable against LGSF-SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting HRMO to:

a. Formulate policy guidelines and updated organizational structure relative to the
hiring/rehiring of JO workers to establish a clear expectation and protect the
rights of both the JO workers and the Municipal Government. Henceforth, ensure
that the hiring of JOs is in accordance with Sections 76 and 77 of RA No. 7160 and
CSC MC No. 17, series of 2002; and
b. Establish a more stringent process for reviewing the accomplishment report and
DTR, as basis for the payment of wages to JO workers to ensure that they are paid
accurately based on actual days worked and reported accomplishments.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant
and Acting HRMO to adjust accordingly the noted discrepancy in the wages of the
two JO workers, henceforth, ensure that the rate paid correspond to the rate stated in
the contracts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
immediately submit to the Audit Team the required documents as stated in Annex H,
to complete the audit of the transactions. Likewise, ensure that supporting
documents are complete and properly verified before payments are made.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the GFPS Focal Point Person and GAD TWG to conduct thorough planning
and analysis to ensure that those identified PPAs to be included in the GPB for CY
2025 are those that are considered by the Municipality as gender activities that can
immediately address the identified gender issues/mandates. Take into consideration
the feasibility and propriety of the proposed PPA before including it in the plan and
exert effort in accomplishing all the targeted PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Supply Officer, End-User and Inspection Officer:
Not to prepare and sign IAR for delivered items which do not conform with the specifications provided in the Purchase Orders so that payment thereof would not be approved;
Provide the actual inspection and acceptance dates in the IAR.
Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassified PPE items in the books of accounts.
We also recommended that the Supply Officer:
Prepare the RPCPPE with complete details, such as specifying the actual condition and location of the reported properties;
Prepare the PCs with complete details, such as providing the date of acquisition/ issue/ transfer/ disposal of PPE; description of PPE; quantity received; quantity issued/ transferred / disposed and the balance as of year-end.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Supply Officer and the PELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the prepared Bank Reconciliation Statements to ensure the accuracy of amounts and balances reported therein.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer verify   the accuracy of the List of Unreleased Checks forwarded to the Municipal Accounting Office to prevent similar errors in the future.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee regularly conduct a physical count of inventories and prepare the required RPCI. Submit to the Audit Team copy of the report within the prescribed period.
We also recommended that the Supply Officer prepare SSMI duly supported by the original copies of RIS on a weekly basis, and submit the same to the Municipal Accounting Office for the recognition of expenditures in the appropriate expenditure accounts.
Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant record expenditures of inventories based on the SSMI prepared and submitted by the Supply Officer.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the supporting documents to prove the validity and propriety of recorded ‘Accounts Payable’ in CY 2024 totaling ₱1,263,936.16. Henceforth, record only in the ‘Accounts Payable’ obligations that are founded on valid claims properly supported by sufficient evidence.

We reiterated our CY 2023 audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant draw journal entries to transfer the Municipality’s 20% share from Fire Code Fees to the Trust Liabilities account.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant expedite the preparation of the final fund utilization reports of all completed PPAs funded by NGAs and to promptly return the unexpended balances to extinguish the Municipality’s liability in the books of accounts, and put the funds to use for other equally important projects of the Source Agencies.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Initiate the confirmation of existence of dormant ‘Due to NGAs’ accounts through sending of confirmation letters to the Source Agencies;
reclassify to ‘Subsidy from National Government’ the remaining National ADAC Award amounting to ₱150,570.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled ‘Due to GSIS’ amounting to ₱100,335.04. Any confirmed unsettled balance must be remitted to the GSIS immediately.
We recommended that the HRM Officer mandate the unregistered Municipal employee to apply for GSIS membership to effect remittance of CY 2024 contributions and ensure that employee receive her entitled benefits and avoid unnecessary financial burden.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled prior years' ‘Due to Pag-IBIG’ account balance of ₱19,932.78. Any confirmed unsettled balance must be remitted to Pag-IBIG immediately; and
Record transactions as they occur in order to provide basis for their recognition in the books. Henceforth, debit the ‘Due to Pag-IBIG’ account only when the remittance of Pag-IBIG contribution is made.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately remit to the Pag-IBIG the total withheld contributions of Ms. Ouano and of the JO personnel which remained unremitted as of this writing.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the Municipal Engineer, MPDC and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs under the   20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation shall be considered; and
Stop charging ineligible expenditure items to the 20% DF in compliance with Item 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.
This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Engineer together with the Municipal Inspectorate Team, ensure 100% completion of the implemented projects before the issuance of the Certificate of Completion and payment of final billing to contractors.

We commended the management on the implementation of disaster mitigation and preparedness related PPAs which enhanced the Municipality’s preparedness to respond during times of calamity or disaster.
On the other hand, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Through the LDRRM Council:
responsively allocate the 70% MF on the four (4) thematic areas of   disaster risk and reduction;
submit a written justification on why payments for the expenditure items presented in Table 3 were charged to the 5% LDRRMF for our proper audit disposition; and
ensure that expenditure items to be charged to the STF-DRRM account are in the annual LDRRMFIP, otherwise prepare a supplemental LDRRMFIP and a revised AIP in accordance with the approved LDRRM Plan before release and utilization of unexpended funds for new expenditure items.
Through the LDRRM Council and Municipal Budget Officer:
stop using appropriation for purposes other than what was approved.  Funds should be spent only for the intended purpose; and
stop charging ineligible expenditure items to the 5% LDRRMF in compliance with Items 5.1 to 5.4 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.
refund the excess disbursement of ₱16,500.00 to the 5% LDRRMF for it was disbursed without authority.
This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the members of the GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) and GAD Focal Person to:
prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
monitor actual expenditures to ensure the Municipality has sufficient funds for implementing all GAD activities and coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant in preparing the annual GAD Utilization Report; and
refrain from charging non-GAD-related activities to the GAD budget thru coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that expenses excluded from the GPB and those not clearly addressing a specific gender issue are not charged to the GAD Budget.

We recommended that the Local School Board, headed by the Municipal Mayor:
stop charging ineligible expenditure items to the SEF in compliance with Item 4 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JMC No. 1, series of 2017.
earnestly submit the quarterly and annual utilization reports of the SEF to the DepEd Central Office through its appropriate channel, copy furnished the local Sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG within the prescribed period; and
post the utilization reports to the FDP online Portal of the Municipality and/ or in at least three (3) conspicuous places within the indicated timelines.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the HRM Officer stipulate in the Job Order worker’s Contract of Service the allowable amount of traveling expenses based on the rates and terms agreed upon by the JO worker and the Municipality, provided that traveling and attendance to meetings/ conferences/ trainings are essential to the effective performance of his/ her functions and it is required to meet the needs of the department/ office.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the wages of JO workers adapt the mandated daily minimum wage rate pursuant to the guidelines stated above.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that travel claims of JO workers are supported with a certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official stating that official missions/ tasks could not be performed by/ or assigned to any other regular/ permanent official and/ or employee; and that the tasks/ activities are necessary to fulfill the obligations as contained in their contract of service.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Immediately compute the correct tax liability for each affected employee and official, encompassing the previously excluded Service Recognition Incentive (SRI) and BAC Honoraria. The corresponding under withheld amount should be reflected in a payroll adjustment and remitted to the BIR as soon as possible, along with any applicable penalties or interest; and
Carefully examine DVs and ensure that taxes from taxable compensations of Municipal employees are duly withhold to avoid the same lapses in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled balance of ₱10,536.42 in the 'Due to PhilHealth' account for prior years. Any confirmed outstanding balance must be remitted to PhilHealth immediately.
We also recommended that the Payroll-In-Charge carefully prepare the monthly payroll to ensure that salaries of Municipal officials and personnel salary are deducted with the correct amounts due for remittance to various  agencies.  The Payroll Officer   should also ensure that these deductions tally with the Employees Premium Remittance List.
Lastly, we recommended that HRM Officer:
ensure the full and timely remittance of the amounts withheld from officials and personnel as well as the share provided by the Municipality in accordance with the deadline set by PhilHealth; and
immediately remit to the PhilHealth the total withheld contributions of Ms. Ouano in accordance with RA No. 11223.

We commended the efforts of the Municipality to insure its properties against fire and all other natural and man-made disaster or calamities.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Supply Officer to:
Prepare annually the consolidated Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties and other assets of the Municipality; and
Submit the consolidated PIF to the Audit Team Leader and the GIF, GSIS, not later than April 30 of each year as required in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage over all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality with the GIF of the GSIS.

We recommended that Management:

a.	Require the CAD to prepare adjustments to:

Record the CIP-Buildings and Other Structures pertaining to the SJMC project of P332,173,902.73 to its proper asset account; and



Record the cost of the demolished buildings /structures in the amount of P151,947,400.99 and the cost of the new buildings/ structures constructed thereat once appraised;




b. Cause the appraisal of the new buildings/ structures constructed in the location of previously reported demolished buildings/ structures as reflected in the RPCPPE; and 



c. Direct the GSD to immediately prepare the required documents for the disposal of unserviceable PPE and submit the request to CAO for evaluation and technical inspection.

We recommended that Management direct the CAD to:

Recognize AP for the completed infrastructure projects in the amount of P93,292,701.54 and record these payables of the City in the prescribed General Journal; and



Require the CAD and GSD to submit to CAO the lacking documents to support the payables of P62,046,856.35.
