5. Require the Supply Officer to update property cards at the time of delivery and reconcile with the ledgers cards of the Municipal Accounting Office.
6. Require the Supply Officer to determine to whom the missing properties were issued. He shall then issue a demand for the production, otherwise the accountable officer should be asked to apply for relief of accountability following prescribed procedures/requirements.
7. Require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify PPE items amounting to P574,783.56 in accordance with the PPSAS Chart of Accounts.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entries to capitalize the (borrowing costs) to correct the balances of the Construction in Progress – Buildings and the Government Equity accounts.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
On Stale Checks:
• direct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to restore the cash and liabilities pertaining to the stale checks to present fairly the recorded cash in bank and accounts payable accounts.
On absence of supporting documents:
• require the Municipal Accountant to submit the documentary evidence to support recording of bank charges and interest expense.
observations.managementAction: Implemented
Implemented
Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to open a specific Subsidiary Ledger of the Loan Domestic availed, interest expense paid, and other expenses related to the loan in compliance to regulations.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
1. The MDRRM Officer to fully and timely implement the PAPs and prepare and submit a report thereon, to ensure that disaster preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery will be achieved and to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disaster;
2. Direct the LDRRM Council to regularly monitor and evaluate its implementation; and
3. The Municipal Accountant to use the disclosure format for MDRRMF in the Notes to financial statement as required.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to implement and monitor implementation of programs and projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund both of the Current and Continuing balances.
b. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the preparation of engineering plans, designs, programs of work, etc. and required documents;
c. Direct other support staff and personnel of the MPDC and Engineering Office to facilitate completion of documents relative to programs and projects funded by the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to record back to Property, Plant and Equipment all public infrastructures that were transferred to the Registry of Public Infrastructure for fair presentation in the financial statements and to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer and General Services Officer in the conduct of an inventory of all public infrastructures of the Municipality and classify the same in accordance with the components provided in COA Circular No. 2016-004.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to exert effort to enforce immediate settlement of the audit disallowances and charges. Likewise, ensure that all employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the disallowances and charges for which they are liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular 2009-006 that the settlement of disallowances and charges is made within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to fully implement the planned programs, activities and projects for disaster preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery so that the MDRRM Fund would be utilized and the intended benefits would be fully achieved.
observations.managementAction: Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to exert effort to enforce immediate settlement of the audit disallowances. Likewise, ensure that all employees who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the disallowances and charges for which they are liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular 2009-006 that the settlement of disallowances and charges is made within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive compel the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Agriculture, Municipal Social Welfare and Planning and Development Officer to fast track the implementation of various projects funded from fund transfers and to facilitate the liquidation of funds as well as the availment of the benefits of the projects by the constituents.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections regularly pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on NGAS, Section 111 of GAAM Vol. I and DBM-DOF Joint Cir. 1-81. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all necessary adjustment collections in order to arrive at correct balance in the balances and adjust cashbooks.
observations.managementAction: Due to Covid-19 Pandemic, the deposits were not regularly made regularly due to work from home arrangements and restrictions of individuals and LBP. The management noted on the recommendation. The Municipal Accountant made the necessary adjustment to reconcile with the balances and adjust cashbooks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to submit the lacking documents to ensure completeness, authenticity and regularity of SAP transactions and in compliance with the above-stated laws and rules. The Municipal Accountant ensure that the grant liquidation of the cash advances are supported with complete and proper documents pursuant to Sec. 4(6) of P.D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The management complied and submitted mostly on the lacking documents except the pictures from Brgy. Lomubang.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, specifically the Municipal Engineer, require the contractor to undertake the remedial measures cited by the TS of COA-CAR. In case the contractor fails to institute the necessary corrective actions, we have also recommended that management implement the remedies provided for in Section 62 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The Contractor corrected the deficiencies of the project and the Management requested for the re-inspection of the project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the members of the committee cause the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise taxes in order to give the tobacco farmers the early enjoyment of the benefits they rightfully deserve.
observations.managementAction: The Notice of Authority to Debit Account issued dated December 17, 2020 debited the funds in the LGU account dated December 21, 2020. Philgeps portal was posting not working since November 25, 2020, also the reason BAC was not able to post in the portal until the issue was fixed on January 15, 2021. As to date, the Tobacco Funds were completed and implemented and being enjoyed by the people.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the goods and supplies be supported with RSMI and list of recipients/beneficiaries duly signed acknowledgement receipt showing the items and quantity actually received by recipients/beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The lacking documents were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the members of the BAC submit justification/explanation on the noted violations and ensure that winning bidders submit complete documentary requirements prior to entering into contract.
observations.managementAction: The project was already awarded to Robustek when their president Rodolfo Abdon died. We feared delay might occur if the project will not proceed, so BAC allowed Robustek to submit its 2017 FS. Robustek promised to fixed their FS pending the election of a new president. The contractor did comply and have already submitted their FS.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management: 

The CAD to:

Conduct thorough review and validation to ensure that the noted PPE items valued at P366,854,654 found per physical count of the IC were actually not taken up in the accounting records before recognizing the same in the books of accounts (Paragraph 1.3);

Reclassify the semi-expendable properties costing below the P15,000 capitalization threshold from PPE accounts to their appropriate inventory.

Adjust the depreciation of the TSE account which was erroneously charged to the ME account (Paragraph 1.7);

Remove from the ICTE account those property items erroneously classified to the said account, and record the same to the appropriate PPE and Inventory accounts. Further, provide details on the adjustment to the ICTE account amounting to P1,506,067 to substantiate the same (Paragraph 1.12);

Conduct investigation/verification on the noted difference in the carrying value of the ME and TSE accounts as per SL, the lapsing schedule and the RPCPPE, and effect the necessary adjustment once the error is identified (Paragraph 1.16); and

Establish/determine the details and composition of the PPE items lumped as beginning balances enumerated in Paragraph 1.24.4.

Instruct the Inventory Committee   to complete the physical count of the City’s PPE on or before September 30, 2021.

Require the CGSD to:

Coordinate with the CAD as regards the details of the PPE items recorded in the books but not included in the RPCPPE, and establish its existence and whereabouts for the appropriate recording of the same in the inventory report; and

Remove from the RPCPPE property items below the P15,000 capitalization threshold and record the same to its appropriate inventory accounts. Likewise, provide a separate report on these semi-expendable properties and ensure that the issuance of the same to the end-user is covered with Inventory Custodian Slip, for accountability purposes (Paragraph 1.5).

Instruct the CAD and CGSD to:

Reconcile regularly their PPE records and identify the details of the discrepancies presented in Paragraph 1.23 and work back on prior years’ transactions to complete the reconciliation

Determine the reason/s on the observed variance in the recorded cost of PPE items in the books as against the RPCPPE as presented in Paragraph 1.24.8 and effect the necessary adjustment to reconcile the same.
observations.managementAction:
Out of the P366,854,654 PPE items found per physical count but not taken in the books, P319,111,771.77 were already identified leaving a balance of P47,742,882.23. The CAD will continue to review and validate the balance of the noted PPE items said to be not taken up in the accounting records.

Semi-expendables PPE classified as Other Machineries and Equipment, Communication Equipment, Military, Police and Scientific Equipment, Other Transportation Equipment, Disaster Risk Response Equipment, Sports Equipment, Office Equipment. Books, Furniture and Fixtures, ICT Equipment and Other PPE accounts amounting to P77,114,628.28 were already identified and reclassified to its appropriate Inventory accounts.

Adjusting journal entries were made to correct the depreciation of the TSE account erroneously recorded to the ME account as per JEV No. 100-2021-05-0094.

Adjusting journal entries were made to properly classify various properties and inventory items erroneously taken as ICTE account as per JEV No. 100-2021-05-0096.

Adjusting journal entries were made to recognize and correct the difference in the carrying value of the ME and the TSE accounts as per JEV No. 100-2021-05-0095.

Since the details and composition of the PPE items lumped as beginning balances totaling P346,196,043.24 could not be established, the CAD will recommend to the CEO to avail the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as per COA Circular No. 2020-06 dated January 31, 2020. 

The GSD had completed the physical count of the remaining properties situated outside the City Hall premises and the corresponding revised RPCPPE was submitted to OCA on July 29, 2021.

The CGSD had coordinated with the CAD to determine the details of the PPE items recorded in the books but not included in the RPCPPE. 

The GSD facilitated the removal of the semi-expendable properties from the RPCPPE and prepared and submitted the inventory report for semi-expendable properties to the Office of the City Auditor on July 29, 2021.

The IC, CAD and GSD will continue to work hand in hand to identify the details of the noted discrepancies and to reconcile the RPCPPE and CAD records.

The GSD and the CAD was able to identify and adjust some of the observed variance in the cost of PPE items. Verification and reconciliation still in process.
observations.reason:
Voluminous records to be reviewed coupled with the lack of manpower to do the reconciliation.








Verification and identification of the semi-expendable items still on-going.










Failure to establish the details and composition of the PPE items lumped as beginning balances due to lack of relevant records.





Failure of the GSD to establish the existence and whereabouts of the subject PPE items.




There were still inventories amounting to P7,200,383.72 erroneously taken up in the books as PPE.





Reconciliation and updating of the CAD and the CGSD records still on-going.




Difficulty in establishing the actual cost of most of the properties due to absence of records.

observations.managementAction:
The City Accountant had instructed the concerned CAD personnel to consistently apply the perpetual inventory method in accounting inventories in compliance with existing accounting standards and policies

Preparation and accomplishment of the SLCs were undertaken by the CAD during the year. 

The IC had conducted the inventory count for office supplies and other supplies and materials inventory accounts amounting to P7,013,195.73 and P2,402,133.09, respectively. The corresponding inventory reports were submitted to the OCA on January 31, 2022.

Preparation and accomplishment of the SCs was   undertaken by the CGSD in compliance with the audit recommendation. 

Regular reconciliation of inventory records between the CAD and the GSD is being undertaken in compliance with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
Lack of review and monitoring by the concerned CAD officials.






Inventory-taking for other inventory accounts not undertaken by the IC.

observations.recommendation:
Require the CTD to:

Issue immediately the notices of delinquency to the concerned delinquent taxpayers as mandated under Section 254 of RA No. 7160; and

Coordinate with the City Legal Office for guidance and assistance in the application of the legal remedies allowed by law to enforce the collection of long overdue or delinquent realty taxes.
observations.managementAction:
Noted as Fully Implemented: 

Notices of Realty Tax Delinquency were prepared and served personally and thru registered mail to the delinquent taxpayers.

Pending the results of the Notice of Delinquency issued to the delinquent taxpayers, consultation with the City Legal Officer have already been conducted by the City Treasurer regarding legal remedies to be instituted to enforce collection of delinquent realty taxes.

observations.recommendation: Management to direct the CAO to regularly undertake tax mapping operations to ensure that all RPU within the City’s jurisdiction are accurately and completely taken up in the AR, for the proper computation and imposition of realty taxes.
observations.managementAction: The CAO had conducted series of field operations during the year and was able to undertake the required updating of the City’s real property records despite the pandemic.

observations.recommendation: The Management to require the CAO to immediately subject the 1,712 RPU to the general revision of the assessed value of real property assessments as mandated under City Ordinance No. 46, series of 2017, for the imposition and collection of appropriate realty taxes due from them.
observations.managementAction: The assessed value of the 1,712 RPUs were already revised by the CAO during the year pursuant to City Ordinance No. 46 series of 2017.

observations.recommendation:
Management to:

Refrain from granting cash advances to accountable officers whose previous cash advances are not yet liquidated/settled; 

Consider designating other accountable officers to handle cash advances for the implementation of other programs and projects of the City; and

Require the CAD:

Ensure that appropriate accounts are used in the recording of cash advances; and

Maintain a separate subsidiary ledger solely for cash advances granted for confidential activities.
observations.managementAction:
The status and immediate liquidation of the cash advances granted to the City’s Accountable Officers were being strictly monitored by the CAD and the CTO, hence, all cash advances granted were liquidated during the year, except advances for CF.

Additional accountable officers were designated to handle cash advances to defray expenses for the implementation of the City’s programs and activities.

Recording of cash advances granted to the accountable officers were properly classified and taken up in the books.

Separate subsidiary ledger was maintained by the CAD for cash advances granted to defray expenses for confidential activities.
observations.reason: There are instances that additional cash advances were granted to the designated Accountable Officers.

observations.managementAction:
The CAD had submitted to the OCA the complete supporting documents of the recorded obligations amounting to P148.182 million on June 4, 2021.

All obligations recorded in the books during the year are properly and completely supported with pertinent documents.

All recorded obligations which had been outstanding for two years or more were already reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund as per JEV Nos. 100-2021-05-0086 and 200-2021-05-030 amounting to P34.053 million and P33,016, respectively.

Additional information relevant to the understanding of the recorded obligations were appropriately disclosed in the Notes to FS.

observations.recommendation: Management to require the concerned   Departments or Offices to immediately implement the programs/projects enumerated in Paragraph 8.2
observations.managementAction: Funds received from the DILG, PHIC and BTR totaling P30,119,157 for the implementation of specific programs/projects were already utilized during the year.
observations.reason: Lack of appropriate action by the concerned offices.

observations.recommendation: Management to return immediately the excess or unexpended balance of the fund transfers totaling P1.312 million to their respective SAs pursuant to Items 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
observations.managementAction: Excess or unexpended balances of fund transfer amounting to P356,928.98 had been returned to the respective SAs.
observations.reason: Difficulty in obtaining the MOAs of the funds downloaded to the City to serve as basis in returning the remaining balance of fund transfer to the concerned SAs.

observations.recommendation: Management to require the CAD to reclassify the funds received from PCSO and PHIC from Due to NGAs account to Due to GOCCs account.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting journal entry was prepared by the CAD reclassifying the funds received from PCSO and PHIC from the Due to NGAs to the Due to GOCCs account as per JEV No. 300-2021-06-0010.

observations.recommendation:
Management to:

Submit the required payroll registers pertaining to the payments of salaries of Job Order personnel thru ATM as enumerated in Paragraph 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001, for verification;

Stop the practice of processing payments for transactions not supported with complete documentation; and

Henceforth, strictly comply with the mandates of Paragraph 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 in the processing of claims.
observations.managementAction:
The subject required payroll registers were already submitted to OCA and has already been audited by the AT.

Payments of salaries of Job Order personnel were supported with complete documentation.

Compliance with Paragraph 4.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 was observed by the City during the year.

observations.recommendation:
To optimize the utilization of the 20% DF, Management to require the SJCDC to:

Adopt a system that will ensure monitoring of the execution of all PPAs to warrant their efficient and timely completion; and

Re-appropriate the unexpended balances of the DF to other development programs/projects and facilitate the submission of the same to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval thru the enactment of an Ordinance.
observations.managementAction:
The City Budget Office closely coordinated with the City Development Council to apprise the latter of the status of 20% IRA Utilization to serve as basis in the monitoring of PPAs implementation.

Unexpended balances and unimplemented projects of the DF were reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the City and re-appropriated to identified development projects as per City Ordinance No. 22 series of 2021 (Supplemental Budget No. 1-2021) dated May 31, 2021.
observations.reason: Delays and problems encountered in the implementation of some of the PPAs.

observations.recommendation: Management to ensure implementation of all planned DRRM PPAs to attain the City’s objectives in mitigating the impacts of calamities. Further, assure that all expenses to be charged against the mitigation fund are included in the approved LDRRMFIP and AIP.
observations.managementAction:
The CDRRMO has implemented 11 planned PPAs during the year. The said Office will continue to do its utmost effort to implement all the planned DRRM PPAs despite the challenges faced by the LGUs in battling the COVID-19 pandemic.

Further, Management had ensured that all expenses charged to the mitigation fund are included in the approved LDRRMFIP and AIP.
observations.reason: Health restrictions due to the outbreak of the Covid-19 pandemic.

observations.managementAction:
The procurement of vaccines for high-risk patients has been undertaken by the City of San Juan during the year. The other two PPAs were already incorporated by the GFPS in CY 2022 GPB. 

The City’s GAD database containing gender statistics and sex-disaggregated data has already been updated and established by the CPDO wherein print out copy of the same was submitted to OCA.

City Ordinance No. 41, series of 2021 had been enacted by the City Council amending the inconsistent provisions of the GAD Code of 2017.

observations.recommendation:
Management to:

Remit immediately the P0.762 million withheld from City personnel in CY 2020, to the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth;

Investigate the complete details of the prior years’ unremitted premiums/contributions totaling P15.540 million in order to expedite the remittance of the same to the concerned agencies; and

Update the information and status of employment of the concerned City officials and employees through regular submission of ARA to the GSIS so that appropriate billings be issued by the latter.
observations.managementAction:
The CAD was unable to remit the amounts of P396,284.65 and P36,854.31 to the GSIS and PhilHealth, respectively, due to incomplete information such as GSIS numbers and PhilHealth account as well as the status update of the employees. The aforementioned reasons were already relayed to CHRRD for their appropriate action. On the other hand, verification of the unremitted Pag-IBIG contributions is still on-going.

The Management hired additional personnel to assist the CAD in establishing the details of the prior years’ unremitted premiums/contributions. Initial investigation revealed an over and under deductions of premiums contributions and loan amortizations with a net variance of P1,328,541.94 had occurred in CY 2016.

The updating of information and status of employment and submission of ARA to the GSIS is on-going.
observations.reason:
Limited manpower resources to do the validation and updating of information and current status of the concerned employees.






Establishing the details of the prior year’s unremitted contributions still on going.




Delay in the updating of information due to lack of personnel.

observations.recommendation: The Management to require the CAD to ensure the timely submission of all DVs and LDs together with sufficient supporting documents to the OCA pursuant to pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: All DVs/LDs with its corresponding supporting documents pertaining to COVID-19 financial transactions for CY 2020 had been transmitted on May 28, 2021 to the OCA and audited by the AT.

observations.recommendation:
That Management:

Submit explanation why the posting requirements were not complied; and

Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of DBM-LBC No. 125 on the posting and reporting requirement as regards the receipt and utilization of the BGCM funds
observations.managementAction:
Valid explanation/justification was submitted by the Management.

The City Government had complied with the posting and reporting requirements required under Section 4 of LBC No. 125.

observations.recommendation: Management to require the CAO to properly tag RPUs in    the RPTAS-Assessment Roll after all the necessary documentary requirements are submitted by the real property owners seeking tax exemption.
observations.managementAction: Proper tagging of RPUs in the Assessment Roll duly supported with pertinent documentary requirements has been undertaken by the CAO.

During the clarificatory meeting held in August 2021, the concerned DSWD-NCR official informed that the funds downloaded pertaining to SLP were in the form of a “Grant” and not a loan.

Verification conducted by the City’s Social Worker disclosed that all small businesses were already closed. Hence, collection of loan amortization from the beneficiaries would be very difficult and/or remote. 

Based on the case study conducted by the CSWDO, the 18 microenterprises were found to be no longer operational due to bankruptcy. A recommendation for the termination of the financial obligations of the subject SLPs was initiated by the said office.

The CAD had coordinated to the CSWDO and is currently in the process of recommending the termination of the financial obligations to the subject SLPAs and to treat the same as income of the City.
observations.reason:
During the clarificatory meeting held in August 2021, the concerned DSWD-NCR official informed that the funds downloaded pertaining to SLP were in the form of a “Grant” and not a loan.




Verification conducted by the City’s Social Worker disclosed that all small businesses were already closed. Hence, collection of the amount due from the concerned beneficiaries is doubtful.




Proper documentation and the necessary adjustment to be made in the books not yet undertaken.

observations.recommendation:
The management to require the:

GSD to dispose the accounted unserviceable properties in accordance with the procedures prescribed by the existing rules and regulations, either through public auction, sale through negotiation, donation or destruction, as the case warrants, to generate additional income therefrom which could be used for other more productive projects; and

CAD to effect the necessary adjusting entries to drop the subject unserviceable properties from the books, after the disposal of the same.
observations.managementAction:
On November 17, 2021, the GSD had submitted letter request duly supported by IIRUP and other relevant documents to the OCA for inspection and disposal of various unserviceable motor vehicles of the City amounting to P12,194,000.00.

The processing of the request for disposal of unserviceable properties is on-going.
observations.reason: The subject unserviceable properties were not yet disposed, hence, the dropping of the same in the books is not feasible.

observations.recommendation:
The Management to:

Strictly enforce collection of outstanding receivables from the City’s officers and employees who are still in the service. For those employees who will resign or retire from the service, effect the deductions from their claims for terminal leave credits.  For those who have already resigned or were retired with unpaid loan balances, issue demand letters for the settlement thereof. Otherwise, require the refund of the amounts from the officials and employees responsible for the grant of clearances;

For cash shortages, enforce the immediate settlement of the same; and

Exert extra effort to provide details or breakdown as to the nature of the account Due from Officers and Employees recorded in the books under the SEF and TF.
observations.managementAction:
The City was able to collect the amount of P33,659.00 from the five former City employees.  

Case has already been filed by the City Legal Office to the three former accountable officers   for the collection of the cash shortages.

The CAD had exerted efforts to determine the details and nature of receivables recorded in the SEF books but to no avail.
observations.reason:
Difficulty in the collection of the remaining dormant receivables since the concerned debtors could no longer be located.




Court hearing is on-going. 




Absence of pertinent record regarding the same.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and MSWDO to submit the lacking documents and explain or correct the deficiencies noted to comply with the DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 9 dated April 9, 2020 in order to ensure that the recipients are qualified beneficiaries and that the financial assistance were actually and fully received by them.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman and the Municipal Accountant to comply and see to it that all the requirements for emergency purchases pursuant to GPPB Circulars, Resolutions and the Government Procurement Reform Act or RA 9184 were followed and attached, to ensure transparency and accountability of procurement process.
observations.managementAction: Emergency procurements were supported with the required documents pursuant to GPPB Circulars.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE require the Acting Municipal Treasurer being the Supply Officer/Property Custodian to observe strictly the provisions stated in Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular 2012-001 and Item No. 3 of COA Circular 81-131-A, and evaluate the condition of the motor vehicle and frequency of previous repairs made before a repair be undertaken again to determine that such repair is more prudent and economical than the procurement of a new motor vehicle.
Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that Disbursement Voucher representing payment for repairs of government vehicles are duly supported with the required documents to avoid possible suspension in audit of the said transaction.
observations.managementAction: Evaluation of condition of motor vehicles being repaired were made before repairs were undertaken.
observations.reason: Some payments for repairs and maintenance of motor vehicles were not completely supported with the required documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with complete documentation in compliance with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 in order to substantiate the legality, validity and propriety of transactions and in order to avoid the issuance of NS.
Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the immediate submission of the lacking supporting documents for audit purposes, thus legality, validity and propriety of the transactions would be ascertained.
observations.managementAction: Lacking documents noted during the post-audit were submitted.
observations.reason: Sixty-nine transactions were paid despite the absence of the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE, thru the Sangguniang Bayan, formulate standard implementing guidelines to regulate the financial aid or assistance granted to various individuals in crisis situation or AICS to avoid inequitable allocation to the constituents of the Municipality. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE instruct the MSWDO and Municipal Accountant to see to it that the minimum documentary requirements are provided prior to the release of AICS and ensure that assistance for a certain patient will not be availed twice by his/her family members. We encourage Management consider the Memorandum Circular No. 11, Series of 2019 issued by the DSWD which sets forth the revised guidelines on the Implementation of AICS.
observations.managementAction: Documentary requirements are provided before the release of AICS.

observations.recommendation:

Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to release payments only when duly acknowledged with completely accomplished official receipt/evidence of receipt of payment and instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure such compliance in accordance with COA Circular No. 2004-006 dated September 9, 2004 and Section 6 of PD No. 1445 to avoid public perception of dishonesty and allowing or tolerating such act.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer submitted an explanation.
observations.reason: Disbursements were supported with duly accomplished official receipts.

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:

Instruct the Local Development Council to immediately look into the unimplemented developmental projects as embodied in the APP and develop feasible and specific solutions to effectively implement those projects.

Instruct the Municipal Engineer to be very prudent, meticulous, conscientious and precise in evaluating the timely and perfect implementation of each and every project. Likewise, conduct regular inspection of projects together with the Project Monitoring Committee so that accomplishment is monitored and evaluated based on the project implementation schedule as stipulated in the APP.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer and her personnel to intensify their collecting power, and strictly impose the provisions of the Municipal Revenue Code and the terms and conditions of the Lease Contracts particularly in the imposition of penalty for the delay or non-payment of market rental fees and advise the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to furnish the Municipal Treasury Office with the complete copies of all the contracts of market stall holders to serve as basis in the increase of monthly rentals to be collected.

We have further recommended that management coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan Members to revisit the Revised Revenue Code for possible revision and modification of some terms and conditions in the collection of market stall rental fees particularly on penalties/surcharges for late payments/non-payments of rentals so that stallholders will be compelled to pay their obligations promptly.

Licenses, Mayor’s permits and contracts were renewed for businesses located in the public market despite non-payment of rentals.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to be strict in renewing the Lease Contracts of the stallholders by securing a clearance or certification from the Municipal Treasurer on the payments made by each stallholder. Coordination be observed between the offices concerned to avoid similar instances in the future.

We have also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer advise the Permits and Licensing Unit under her Office to closely monitor the payments of the market stall owners before renewing their license and permits and see to it that the business index cards are regularly updated.

Finally, the Municipal Government be more responsible in the administration and management of the public market operations in order to make the public market more responsive and effective instrument of public service, as well as dynamic and viable enterprise to strengthen its economic potential, with the ultimate goal of contributing significantly to local and national development.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Management direct the BAC and TWG to:

Strictly scrutinize the sufficiency of the winning bidder’s manpower and equipment on all the projects to be awarded and not on an individual project, only in cases where the latter is the lowest bidder in several projects which have similar or overlapping implementation period. The winning contractor is required to supplement its manpower and/or equipment to be utilized in the projects awarded to it or to disqualify it from obtaining several awards or contracts in conformity with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

Efficiently evaluate during the bid evaluation and post qualification the accuracy of the List of All Ongoing Government and Private Contracts and the NFCC of the bidders who were awarded several projects if they accurately reflect its financial capacity to absorb the additional works and to finance its implementation or completion. 



We have recommended further that Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Officer to strictly and closely monitor all projects simultaneously implemented by the contractors to check, verify and evaluate the quality of work and efficiency of the said contractors.
observations.managementAction:
Page 112
of AAR 2019 | The BAC through the TWG conducted post-qualification on the winning bidders. 






The BAC and its TWG ensure that bid evaluation and post qualification on the accuracy of the List of all Ongoing government and private contracts and the NFCC of the bidders who were awarded with the several projects to prevent overlapping periods of implementation to the same contractor at least within the LGU.
