observations.recommendation:
1.6 We have recommended that Management:

a. Require the reconciliation of property and accounting records on every PPE account
classification and effect the necessary adjustments, if any;
b. Require the Property Unit to maintain complete, accurate and up-dated Property
Cards (PCs) and reconcile the same regularly with the Property, Plant and Equipment
Ledger Cards (PPELCs) maintained by the Accounting Office;
c. Require the property custodian to file the PARs/AREs of all movable non-
expendable properties and cause the renewal of the same every January of the 3rd year
after issue in order to facilitate the physical count of PPE and to easily identify the
official/employee responsible in case of loss, resignation, or expiration of term of
office;
d. Require the posting of property tag/number on the prominent portion of every
movable non expendable property/equipment, and
e. Proper disposal of unserviceable properties be made.

2.6 We have recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide to the
Municipal Accountant the complete description and cost segregation of
road components for road projects. Also, the accounting and reporting
guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in
COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated Nov. 23 2015 be strictly complied.
Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for
roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require
the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and
Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for
all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee
to conduct the Physical Count and prepare the Report on the Physical
Count of the Road Network System.

3.4 We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer in coordination
with the Municipal Assessor prepare and furnish the Municipal
Accountant with a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and
the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card
or computer generated report to provide an accurate and reliable basis in
the setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education
taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in
the financial statements.

4.5 We have recommended that Management formulate additional strategies
to intensify and facilitate tax collections. If need be, avail of remedies
embodied in Section 174 of RA 7160 to ensure collection of taxes due the
municipal government in order to increase the collection efficiency.
Further, we have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the
Certified List of All Property Tax Delinquencies to include all tax
delinquents and reconcile the same with the accounting records.

5.7 We have recommended that the local government unit require concerned
officials and employees to submit the monthly accounts covering receipts
and collections and financial reports within reglementary period.

6.12 We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit all
collections regularly pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on NGAS,
Section 111 of GAAM Vol. I and DBM-DOF Joint Cir. 1-81.

6.13 We also have recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all
collections in order to arrive at correct balances and adjust balances in
order to reconcile with the balance in the cashbooks.

7.4 We have recommended that the Local Government Unit Personnel
Officer to check on submitted DTRs upon submission and require all
employees concerned to submit properly filled up daily time records with
complete attachments as indicated in the daily time records submitted.

8.4 We have reiterated our recommendation that Management submit
perfected copy of the government contracts to COA within five working
days from perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001 to
enable timely auditorial and technical review.

9.11 We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
officials to submit the lacking documents to ensure completeness,
authenticity and regularity of SAP transactions and in compliance with
the above-stated laws and rules. The Municipal Accountant also ensure
that the grant and liquidation of the cash advances are supported with
complete and proper documents pursuant to Sec. 4(6) of P.D. 1445.

10.13 We have recommended that the:











































- Municipal Accountant submit the noted lacking documents for the
audit team to evaluate and establish the validity of the disbursements;
- Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure that all documentary
requirements supporting all procurement and disbursements are
present; and;
- Processing and payment of claims that are not properly documented
should be discontinued.

11.5 We have recommended that Management, specifically the Municipal
Engineer, require the contractor to undertake the remedial measures
cited by the TS of COA-CAR. In case the contractor fails to institute the
necessary corrective actions, we have also recommended that
management implement the remedies provided for in Section 62 of the
2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

12.6 We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC)
comply with the rules and regulations as required by the 2019 Revised
IRR of RA 9184.

13.3 We have recommended that the members of the BAC ensure that
winning bidders submit complete documentary requirements prior to
entering into contract.

14.5 We have recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee justify the
noted lapses. Henceforth comply with the rules and regulations as
required by the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

15.7 We have recommended that the members of the BAC ensure that
prospective bidders submit complete eligibility requirements and post the
necessary bid security before they are marked as “passed” and “post-
audited”.

16.6 We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the prompt
implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise
taxes in order to give the tobacco farmers who worked hard for the
generation of the funds the early enjoyment of benefits they rightfully
deserve.

17.5 We have recommended that goods and supplies be supported with RSMI
and list of recipients/beneficiaries duly signed acknowledgement receipt
showing the items and quantity actually received by recipients/beneficiaries.

18.5 We have recommended that the Municipal Government strictly comply
with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated
February 22, 2017 and ensure the effective and efficient implementation
of the proposed projects within the targeted completion period through
close monitoring and supervision to maximize the development benefits
and the early enjoyment of the benefits thereof by the intended
beneficiaries.

We reiterated our recommendation for the Municipal Accountant to prepare
and submit the required monthly BRS to the Audit Team within ten days from
receipt of the bank statements after the end of each month in accordance with
Sections 3.1 and 3.2 COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count on movable properties and the
Accounting Office and Property Division to maintain their respective property
records and reconcile with the results of the actual count. We further recommended
that the Municipal Mayor designate another Property Custodian in view of the
appointment of the former custodian as Bookkeeper.

We recommended that Management require the assigned personnel of the
requisitioning offices to submit copies of distribution lists duly acknowledged by the
recipients to validate actual distribution. The distribution lists should contain the
name and signature of recipients as well as the date of receipt, and such other
necessary information, including the kind and quantity of items received to facilitate
accounting for the food supplies, medicines and other medical supplies and to
establish accountability.

We recommended that Management require the personnel concerned or the
end-user duly authorized by the Head of the PE to properly and completely fill up
the relevant and required information in the POs to allow comparison with the
prevailing market prices in the locality, or with the existing price data of the agency,
the Department of Trade and Industry or other relevant agencies.

We recommended that Management specifically describe the projects and
activities for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan and always secure the
approval or authorization of the Sangguniang Bayan before incurring expenditures
and/or entering into contract for the implementation of projects described in
generic terms in the appropriation ordinance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the
guidelines prescribed in the recording of the receipt and utilization of subsequent
fund releases from the national government to facilitate monitoring and tracking of
grant’s utilization.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit documents to
support payments of cash assistance to LSIs and OFW, such as SB resolution
establishing the guidelines on the identification/assessment of qualified beneficiaries
and rates of financial assistance, authorization letters for claimants receiving
payments in behalf of the intended beneficiaries, and proof of receipt by the latter.

We recommended that Management refrain from holding
seminars/workshops outside of the province in accordance with pertinent sections of
COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012 to avoid extravagant
expenditures and wastage of government funds.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the following:
 The General Services Officer conduct annually the physical count of all Property,
Plant and Equipment and reconcile RPCPPE with the accounting records to fully
identify the discrepancies noted. Complete all the fields required in the RPCPPE
form and the necessary remarks. With regard to disposal, complete the necessary
documents and perform the subsequent procedures in accordance with the Circular.
Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are fully supported with Property Cards and updated
Property Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment by the GSO; and
 The Accounting Department continue all the efforts to secure the necessary
documents to fully identify the PPEs included in the beginning/unidentified
balances to be able to reconcile with the Inventory Report of the GSO. The
Municipality may also refer to COA Circular No. 2020-006, dated January 31, 2020
for the procedures in derecognition of PPEs with no available records of
accountability.
 The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal
Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs
particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures
accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and
verified.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
following:
 The Inventory Committee headed by the General Services Office conduct the
physical count of its Local Road Network and prepare the RPCLRN. Make use of
the template provided in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008 or Appendix 63 of
the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and complete the necessary
information in every field. Submit the accomplished RPCLRN to the Accounting
Office for reconciliation in the books
 The Municipal Engineer provide cost segregation of every road networks owned
by the Municipality per road component for the prior years. Submit the road
networks duly segregated per road component to the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal GSO; and
 The Municipal Accountant provide strategies to identify the breakdown of
beginning balances of Road networks. As to the identified Road Network projects,
coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to provide individual cost per road
component to be the basis in the computation of depreciation expenses.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal General Services Officer reconcile their records to fairly present
the inventory accounts in the Municipality’s financial statements as of December 31,
2020.

Likewise, for the Municipal General Services Officer to immediately effect the
reconciliation in the annual report on the physical count of inventories.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant consider some of the most
important qualitative characteristics of the financial reports of the public sector that
make them useful to the users namely: relevance, faithful representation,
understandability, timeliness, comparability and verifiability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant present in the financial
statements of special accounts public market and slaughterhouse the cost of
depreciation of these facilities and the total book value of these structures to provide
adequate information as to assets, liabilities and equity and be able to determine
whether the economic enterprises have become financially self-sustaining
establishments.

We recommended that the Municipality implement changes in its Annual
budget pursuant to Articles 417 and 418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations
of RA No. 7160.

Also, we recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee Chairman, Vice-
Chairman and its members fully justify the rice procurement with Primavera Ricemill
Corporation that showed price variance of ₱40.60 per kilogram of rice or a total sum
of ₱3,349,500.00 and that the total sum has not to be the subject of audit disallowance.

We further recommended that in-kind welfare assistance be implemented
considering the practices of social welfare and development department that target
beneficiaries are explicitly identified and sex-disaggregated data generated to
effectively address its welfare objectives.

Likewise, we recommended that procurement guidelines in using the procurement mode
emergency or negotiated procurement be adhered to particularly Government
Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Circular 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 on the
prescribed project requirement and price negotiation activity.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee Chairman, Vice-
Chairman and its members fully justify the rice procurement with Primavera Ricemill
Corporation that showed price variance of ₱40.60 per kilogram of rice or a total sum
of ₱6,140,750.00 and that the total sum has not to be the subject of audit disallowance.

We recommended the Municipality submit the necessary supporting
documents of those transactions were deficiencies are noted such as:
Procurement of Rice for Distribution
 Evaluation Report on the selection of the target beneficiaries
 Distribution scheme implemented by the Municipality
 Distribution List showing the final acceptance from the households
 Documentation on actual distribution of rice
Food Packs and Drinking Water during Checkpoints
 Work Program/Project Proposal on the conduct of checkpoint
 Documentation on the distribution of food packs to volunteers
 Documentation on actual check points conducted and accomplishment report
Procurement of IEC Materials
 Photos of actual IEC materials Procured
 Documentation on the activities where IEC materials were used
Also, ensure that succeeding disbursements are completely documented specially to
those pertaining to PPAs which post-monitoring is essential to determine if the goals are
attained by the Municipality.
As to those inconsistencies noted, provide justification to identify the reasons on those
observations, summarized below, as follows:
 Documentation relative to the distribution of food packs for different checkpoint dates
 Price variances on the procurement of PPEs, medicines and supplies Covid-19
response
 Procurement through shopping for items above ₱100,000.00, not considered as
commonly-used supplies
 Supporting documents on the subsistence made during progress monitoring report
conducted by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipality provide justification letter for the
observations noted on the implementation of SAP – ESP to the audit team. Likewise,
ensure that the terms, rules and regulations on the succeeding social amelioration
programs are strictly followed and implemented by the Municipality to ensure timely
delivery of relief to the constituents.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson,
Vice-Chairperson and its members fully justify the awarded procurements to
Sinoequip Incoporated for the delivery of one (1) unit of brand new Sinotruk Howo A7
dump truck amounting to ₱3,980,000.00 and one (1) unit of brand new HT 135W
Hunter Wheel Excavator in the amount of ₱5,500,000.00 which showed price
variances of ₱800,000.00 and ₱2,694,395.00, respectively compared to the same sold
units to Sumitomo Mitsui Construction Co., Ltd. awarded on November 20, 2020 per
submitted Statement of Completed Private and Government Contracts and to the
prevailing market prices of similar units of the same or higher specifications and that
the total resulting price variance has not to be the subject of audit disallowance.

We also recommended that the Committee fully justify the awarded
procurement to Maravilla Trading for the delivery of one (1) unit Dump Truck – Model
H3 amounting to ₱2,595,000.00 which showed price variance of ₱745,000.00
compared to the prevailing market prices of similar units of the same or higher
specifications and that the observed price difference has not to be the subject of
audit disallowance.

We further recommended that the Municipality abide with the provisions of
Section 12 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act
No. 9184 that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) shall only recommend the award
of the contract and not award it to the qualified contractor or supplier.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with the
provisions of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 of the Department of Education,
Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local
Government and prepare the annual and supplemental budget as prescribed for a
more transparent and accountable reporting.

We further recommended that the Board effectively utilize the Fund with the
objective of providing quality elementary and secondary education in the Municipality
and abide with the provisions of procurement Act that the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) shall only recommend the award of the contract and not award it to
the qualified contractor or supplier.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects
that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes. Any delay in the completion of development projects being
implemented by the Municipality definitely slow down the community development or
realization of development objectives intended for the constituents.

We also recommended that the development programs/projects/activities shall
be planned in itemized appropriations in the municipal budget and in the municipal
annual investment program as well as in its Local Development Investment Program
specifying therein projects proposed for inclusion in the local government budget
and any changes/reversions/realignments shall be made pursuant to Articles 417 and
418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160.

Based on the forgoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office
(MDRRMO) to implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under 70%
Mitigation Fund within the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of services and
benefits to the constituents.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General
Services Officer to see to it that in-kind donations are accounted and reported
consistently and strictly follow the required templates and forms prescribed in COA
Circular.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly follow the guidelines provided
in Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2019-01 dated May 17, 2019 to ensure
amended rates of fees or charges are reasonable and just sufficient to recover the
direct fixed cost and variable costs of rendering services and also create a local
government unit with business-friendly environment.

We recommended that the Municipality immediately address the remaining
obstructing issues to the final completion of the housing project. The lots and the
housing units must now be sold or awarded to the intended beneficiaries to prevent
further losses on the part of the Municipality and disappointment to the low-income
earner municipal employees of the Municipality of San Juan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Property Custodian and Municipal Accountant to prioritize the reconciliation of the results of the physical count of the property and the accounting records. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of PPE, after which prepare a duly and completely accomplished RPCPPE using the prescribed format to facilitate reconciliation of the same with the accounting records.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to locate all and make a compilation of any records that could be retrieved and prepare the corresponding assessment/evaluation report as regards the long outstanding liabilities to support the possible reversion of the accounts payable to unappropriated surplus in order not to overstate the liability accounts.  Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to attach the compiled documents including a photocopy of the subsidiary ledgers to the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) representing the reverted accounts payable so that in the event that there may be claims in the future, the JEV would serve as the basis in requesting for a supplemental budget for the settlement of the payable.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Assessor and officials concerned to prioritize the transfer of ownership over these parcels of land acquired by the Municipality to avoid exposing the same to possible third party claims, land disputes and potential loss of government funds.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Head of Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical inventory of supplies held for distribution or consumption and ensure prompt submission of duly and completely accomplished RPCI not later than January 31 of each year as provided in Sections 194, Volume I of GAM in order to ascertain the fair presentation of the account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and MSWDO to submit the lacking documents and explain or correct the deficiencies noted to comply with the DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 9 dated April 9, 2020 in order to ensure that the recipients are qualified beneficiaries and that the financial assistance were actually and fully received by them.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman and the Municipal Accountant to comply and see to it that all the requirements for emergency purchases pursuant to GPPB Circulars, Resolutions and the Government Procurement Reform Act or R.A. 9184 were followed and attached, to ensure transparency and accountability of procurement process.

We recommended that the LCE require the Acting Municipal Treasurer being the Supply Officer/ Property Custodian to observe strictly the provisions stated in Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular 2012-001 and Item No. 3 of COA Circular 81-131-A, and evaluate the condition of the motor vehicle and frequency of previous repairs made before a repair be undertaken again to determine that such repair is more prudent and economical than the procurement of a new motor vehicle.
Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that Disbursement Voucher representing payment for repairs of government vehicles are duly supported with the required supporting documents to avoid possible suspension in audit of the said transaction.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with complete documentation in compliance with Section 4 (6) of P.D. No. 1445 in order to substantiate the legality, validity and propriety of transactions and in order to avoid the issuance of NS. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the immediate submission of the lacking supporting documents for audit purposes, thus legality, validity and propriety of the transactions would be ascertained.

We recommended that the LCE, thru the Sangguniang Bayan, formulate standard implementing guidelines to regulate the financial aid or assistance granted to various individuals in crisis situation or AICS to avoid inequitable allocation to the constituents of the Municipality. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE instruct the MSWDO and Municipal Accountant to see to it that the minimum documentary requirements are provided prior to the release of AICS and ensure that assistance for a certain patient will not be availed twice by his/her family members. We encourage Management consider the Memorandum Circular No. 11, Series of 2019 issued by the DSWD which sets forth the revised guidelines on the Implementation of AICS.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer and suppliers to submit explanation on unfilled out Official Receipts accepted and issued, respectively. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to release payments only when duly acknowledged with completely accomplished official receipt/evidence of receipt of payment and instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure such compliance in accordance with COA Circular No. 2004-006 dated September 9, 2004 and Section 6 of P.D. No. 1445 to avoid public perception of dishonesty and allowing or tolerating such act.

We recommended that the LCE require the drivers or any municipal personnel concerned to observe the complete and proper accomplishment of Driver’s Trip Tickets pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 to provide basis in determining the reasonableness of fuel consumption of each municipal vehicle.

We recommended that the LCE instruct and remind the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the timely submission of accounts in order to facilitate immediate review, evaluation and examination of consummated transactions. Likewise, we recommended that the LCE instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to impose internal rules in the movement or flow of transactions, like the claiming of checks, in order to expedite submission of reports and documents.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to furnish the list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities and those that are to be implemented during the year be given at the beginning of the year to the Office of the Auditor.  Also, provide a notice to the Office of the Auditor within ten days, after the award of infrastructure projects or before its start that the project signboard was already posted. If personal submission of said list or notice is not possible, the duly signed scanned copy of the list or notice could be submitted to the official email address of the Audit Team (r108agb@yahoo.com). Likewise, it is encouraged that Management attach pictures of the posted signboards taken in the project site in the Disbursement Voucher.

We recommended that the LCE instruct Supply Officer or the official responsible in accepting deliveries to furnish a notice to the Office of the Auditor on the deliveries of procured items within 24 hours from the acceptance to comply with the requirement of COA Circular No. 96-010. If personal submission of said notice is not possible, the duly signed scanned copy of the notice could be submitted thru the official email address of the Audit Team (r108agb@yahoo.com) to enable the latter to conduct inspection of procured items to determine whether or not such deliveries conformed with the quantity, quality and specifications in the Purchase Order.  Thus, in case of any discrepancies, Management be immediately informed for its appropriate and immediate action. Likewise, it is encouraged that Management attach pictures of the items delivered in the Disbursement Voucher.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC Secretariat to facilitate the immediate submission of authenticated copies of mandatory documents for audit purposes. We also recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC Chairman that the required mandatory documents be specified on the RFQs as well as the stage of the procurement process when these should be submitted to ensure that procurements are made only from legally eligible suppliers.

We recommended the LCE instruct the BAC Chairman to carefully examine the transactions of the Municipality in order to correctly recommend to the Head of Procuring Entity (HOPE) the alternative mode of procurement to be applied in compliance with the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 to assure the qualification of the suppliers and/or expedite the procurement process.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC Chairman to carefully examine the procurements of goods or services of the Municipality in order to consolidate those with similar nature to avoid splitting of contracts which is strictly prohibited in the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and to ensure that the most advantageous price for the Government is obtained.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant to require the contractor of future infrastructure projects to post duly dated bank guarantee bond equivalent to 10% contract price in lieu of the 10% retention money purposely to cover all defects that may be detected and adhere with the guidelines, policies, rules and regulations under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC Chairman and Secretariat, as a support staff of the BAC, to see to it that the Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed are posted in the PhilGEPS website within the prescribed period of 3 and 15 calendar days in order to ensure procurement transparency.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer and MPDC to prioritize the implementation of the funded projects out of the share of the Municipality from the tobacco excise taxes through maximum use of all available resources so that benefits will be timely delivered to the tobacco farmers/constituents.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the MPDC to ensure the full implementation of the priority projects which remained unimplemented in 2020 and ensure that 20% Development Funds be optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic developmental and environmental outcomes of the LGU in accordance with the DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the GAD Focal Person to maximize the utilization of the GAD Funds through the implementation of GAD-related programs and projects in order to fully attain the objective for which the funds were provided.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to give preferential attention to the retrieval of records and determination of the intended purpose of the transferred funds for possibilities of implementing the projects/activities. In cases that the purposes were already accomplished, we recommended the LCE require the immediate remittance of the balances to the Bureau of Treasury or return the unutilized funds to the source agencies to finally end any obligation created by these transfers and to the fully liquidate these accounts.
In addition, we recommended Management consider Section 2.9 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2 dated December 4, 2020 on the utilization of unexpended cash balances of public funds held in trust by LGUs for purposes that have been completed or abandoned may be transferred by the LGUs concerned to the respective general funds which could be made available for appropriation to support local government programs and projects in response to the Covid-19 pandemic.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the LDRRMO to incorporate in the LDRRMFIP the unexpended balance of the previous LDRRMF, both the continuing appropriation and special trust fund, to ensure the most possible available funds for disaster risk reduction. Likewise, it is recommended that unutilized LDRRMF after five years be reverted back to the General Fund to be used for other social services.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant and the LDRRMO to prepare and submit respectively, the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and submit to the Office of the Auditor to comply with COA Circular No. 2012-002 in order to facilitate timely review, verification and monitoring of the utilization of the said fund. If personal submission of said report is not possible, the duly signed scanned copy of the report could be submitted thru the official email address of the Audit Team (r108agb@yahoo.com).

We recommended the LCE to:
Obtain the prior authorization of the Sanggunian before acting on behalf of the Municipality especially on fiscal matters involving the acceptance and utilization of donated funds.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize the unrecorded cash donations amounting to ₱310,000.00 and its subsequent utilization in the Trust Fund Book;
Ensure that the details of cash donations are disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statements as required under Item VII (2)(c) of COA Circular No. 2014-002;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to adhere with the accounting and reporting guidelines of donations required under Section 5.1.12 of Item V and Annex C of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Item VII(2)(c) of COA Circular No. 2014-002; and
Ensure compliance with the laws on procurement embodied in RA 9184 and keep     themselves abreast with the latest issuances of the GPPB especially on emergency procurement so as to not repeat the deficiencies noted.

We recommended that Management strengthen the data gathering and collating mechanism of the Municipality’s CBMS and GAD Databases through preparation of master plan indicating the timelines, process, budget and other necessary actions. Thereafter, start the implementation in CY 2021 in order to ensure updated, operational and reliable information provided by said databases which will serve the Municipal Government in attaining an evidence-based planning, policy-making, and relief distribution in the future.

Additionally, we recommended that Management augment the number of employees responsible for the maintenance and development of the databases, more specifically to update the data contained therein as data gathering and collating mechanism of the GAD and CBMS Databases is certainly a tedious work.

Lastly, we recommended that Management coordinate with data-gathering government agencies the Municipal’s need for information related to update of databases.  And if necessary, perhaps the Municipality could enter into a partnership or an agreement with such agency, to hasten the retrieval or gathering of data and information.

We recommended that the LCE direct the following:
Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of LRN and prepare the Report of Physical Count of the Road Network System with complete details, and cost segregation and description for road components for road projects.
Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the GSO with a complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to determine, or if not practicable, estimate the carrying amount of the assets replaced taking into consideration the cost, age and condition of the replaced asset.
Municipal Accountant to derecognize the carrying amount of the replaced assets.
Management commented that they will initiate the physical inventory and coordination among the concerned departments will be enjoined. Further, they have agreed to comply with the recommendations to establish the reported amount of the LRN account.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Engineer to provide copies of Certificate of Completion and completely signed Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA) to the Municipal Accountant upon the completion of a certain project that would be her basis in recognizing the completed project in the books.
Municipal Accountant to:
adjust the PPE accounts recognizing additional completed PPE in CY 2020 amounting to ₱7,863,488.54;
adjust the CIP accounts to recognize the correct amount of the completed projects, reclassify the same to the appropriate PPE accounts and recognize corresponding depreciation; and
maintain complete subsidiary ledgers for each PPA for proper recording and monitoring;
Management through the Municipal Accountant took note of the necessary adjustments to be made and recommendation to be complied with. Coordination with the Municipal Engineer is also ensured specifically in determining the physical status of the projects for the proper recognition in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Budget Officer to confer with the Municipal Accountant during the budget preparation to ensure that appropriate and correct accounts are made relative to the nature of the expenditures as PPAs to be implemented.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the disclosure requirements of IPSAS by adequately providing a concise narrative on significant changes and breakdown of material balances to ensure that relevant information is available to the intended users of the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the following concerned heads of offices:
Municipal Engineer to:
Closely coordinate with barangays and Assessor’s Office in the monitoring of construction/improvement of buildings with no permit;
Furnish the Assessor’s Office copies of issued Building Permit and Certificate of Occupancy Permit, as well as complete building plans as reference and support in their inspection and appraisal; and
Monitor the issuance of building and occupancy permits.
Municipal Assessor to:
Conduct field inspection in coordination with the Office of the Municipal Engineer and Bureau of Fire Protection; and
Comply with Section 6, Chapter IV of MRPAAO and require the Municipal Engineer copies of Certificate of Occupancy Permit issued as reference and support in the appraisal of buildings.
The LCE recognizes the importance of maximizing the revenue collections and commented that he will instruct the Office of the Municipal Engineer to regularly provide the data needed by the Municipal Assessor as reference in the appraisal of buildings. Moreover, close coordination with the concerned offices will be enjoined to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the LCE, as chairman of the LSB, to direct the members of the Municipal Local School Board to ensure that funds allocated for SEF be budgeted and utilized strictly in accordance with R.A. No. 7160, DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01, s. 2017 and LOI No. 1462 in order to attain the intended purpose for which the fund was created. We have further recommended that the amount of ₱1,120,000.00 be charged against the available appropriations under the General Fund.

Management commented that they will refer to the said Joint Circular as guide in their planning and budgeting of SEF. As they are not aware of the revised guidelines, they agreed to the recommendation and ensure compliance of the same for the ensuing year.

We recommended the LCE through the BAC and TWG to conduct strict, extensive and efficient bid-evaluation and post-qualification by scrupulously scrutinizing the documents submitted by the bidder particularly the Statement of All On-going Government and Private Contracts, List of Contractor’s Personnel of contractors and List of Contractor’s Equipment.

Management favorably accepted the observation and agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended the Management fast track the implementation of the Sanitary Landfill in order to address the environmental concerns on solid waste management and to continue its efforts towards a more effective and efficient arm of the government in conserving natural resources.

Management commented that transfer to the name of the LGU of the 1.8 hectare lot for the Sanitary Landfill is now on-going and the rest is under negotiation. Representations with the PGLU has already been made to finalize their agreement in financing the Construction of Sanitary Landfill. They assured the Audit Team to fast track the implementation of this project as recommended.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Inventory Team to finish/complete the conduct of the annual physical inventory of PPE by type and submit a  Report on the Physicial Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) in the prescribed format not later than January 31 of each year.  Reconcile the RPCPPE with the ledger balances.
Require the Supply Officer to prepare a separate Inventory Report on obsolete, unserviceable/damaged and beyond repair PPEs using the Waste Material Report (WMR-Appendix 68) and  Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IRRUP-Appendix 67) of the GAM for LGUs.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to drop from the books disposed items using the original copy of the WMR and IRRUP when properly authorized as basis for adjusting the accounts affected by means of a journal entry voucher;
Require the Inventory Team to assign property number to each PPE Inventory to facilitate validation and ascertain existence;
Require the Supply Officer to update property cards  at the time of delivery and reconcile  with the ledgers cards of the Municipal Accounting Office.
Require the  Supply Officer to determine to whom the missing properties were issued. He shall then issue a demand for the production, otherwise the accountable officer should be asked to apply for relief of accountability following prescribed procedures/requirements.
Require the Municipal Accountant to reclassify PPE items amounting to P574,783.56 in accordance with the PPSAS Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entries to capitalize the bank charges (borrowing costs) to correct the balances of the Construction in Progress – Buildings and the Government Equity accounts.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor:
On Stale Checks:
direct the Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to restore the cash and liabilities pertaining to the stale checks to present fairly the recorded cash in bank and accounts payable accounts.
On absence of supporting documents:
require the Municipal Accountant  to submit the documentary evidence to support recording of bank charges and interest expense.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The Supply Officer or personnel concerned to see to it that Purchase Orders/Letter Order/Contracts be properly accomplished specially the terms of delivery and payment; and
The Supply Officer or personnel concerned that the date of acceptance by the supplier in the conforme portion of the Purchase Order is indicated as basis for the imposition of penalty once the supplier failed to deliver its oblligations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to open a specific Subsidiary Ledger of the Loan Domestic availed, interest expense paid, and other expenses related to the loan in compliance to regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
The MDRRM Officer to fully  and timely implement the PAPs and prepare and submit a report thereon, to ensure that disaster preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery will be achieved and to avoid, lessen or limit the potential adverse impact of disaster;
Direct the LDRRM Council to regularly monitor and evaluate its implementation; and
The Municipal Accountant to use the disclosure format for MDRRMF in the Notes to financial statement as required.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to implement and monitor implementation of programs and projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund both of the Current and Prior Years continuing balances.
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the preparation of engineering plans, designs, programs of work,  etc. and required documents;
Direct other support staff and personnel of the MPDC and Engineering Office to facilitate completion of documents relative to programs and projects funded by the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that Management, thru the Accounting Office reclassifies the Market account to Investment Property, Buildings account to conform with IPSAS 16- Investment Property, for fair presentation of financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management will look into the physical and reconcile with the financial records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer trace back the source of the discrepancies of the Cash in Bank account balances and record all book reconciling items to bring the balances into agreement so that the correct balance of cash is presented in the financial statements; b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer verify from the bank the details of all debit and credit memos reflected in the bank statements to record correctly these transactions; and c. verified reconciling items should be taken up immediately after the preparation of the monthly reconciliation statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management have already reconciled and adjusted the unaccounted cash.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposits all collections regularly and to deposit the balance of ₱201,631.35 as of December 31, 2019 pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on NGAS, Section 111 of GAAM Vol. I and DBM-DOF Joint Cir. 1-81. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer records collections and deposits daily in order to distinguish any unrecorded and unremitted to arrive at correct balances. Moreover, the Municipal Treasurer should be vigilant enough in recording collections to avoid erroneous entry that may result to erroneous balances of collections.
observations.managementAction: The Management have already improved their process and trying their best to comply to the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer instruct the collectors and all other local accountable officers to maintain and use cashbooks for proper monitoring of the collections and remittances, thus, precluding possible mishandling and misappropriation of collections pursuant to the requirements of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.
observations.managementAction: The Management have already improved their process and trying their best to comply to the recommendations. As at the moment, each collector of the Treasury is now maintaining their own daily cashbooks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Municipal Accountant submits the noted lacking documents for the audit team to evaluate and establish the validity of the disbursements; b. Municipal Accountant ensures that all documentary requirements supporting all procurement and disbursements are present; and; c. Municipal Accountant discontinue the processing of claims that are not properly documented.
observations.managementAction: Some of the documents were already submitted and management committed to improve their process.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Government stops the practice of allowing job orders to claim per diems. When these employees are directed to perform tasks that require them to travel, they should be allowed to claim actual fare only.
observations.managementAction: The Management have discontinued this practice and will comply with the recommendations. Receipts of payment of disallowances were already collected from the employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management justifies why the prize received was not deposited intact and why was the succeeding deposit made only after ten months.
observations.managementAction: The Management collected and deposited the remaining amount from the PESO in the trust fund. The fund shall be used only for the improvement of the PESO officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality establish specific guidelines in the grant of financial assistance to the indigents. The Audit Team suggested that said guidelines be patterned on the DSWD Administrative Order No. 5 series of 2008.
observations.managementAction: The Management has come up with guidelines as approved by the Sangguniang Bayan patterned under the DSWD Administrative Order No. 5 series of 2008.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management justifies the above noted lapses and ensures compliance with the minimum standards on building specifically on service delivery to guarantee that support is established during disaster emergencies.
observations.managementAction: The features, primarily the building plan was given by the DPWH who funded the project. The evacuation building based on the building plan is designed properly so as to ensure effective and efficient disaster response and recovery services during emergencies. During the exit conference, COA has noted that the location building in Brgy. Badas is hazardous because it is located near Malanas River. However, on management part, it is our humble belief that the building is safe. DPWH who funded the building would have not allowed its construction there. If upon their ocular inspection, the place is unsafe. This is further manifested through their prior construction and funding of a Multi-Purpose Building (Gymnasium) on the site. Lastly, the site is elevated and statements from Brgy Badas Captain Drexell Jay Bose shoes that waster would not reach the building even during typhoon Lawin and Ompong because of the flood control constructed by the municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management justify the noted lapses.
observations.managementAction: First, the management extends apology regarding the matter. We admit our failure to scrutinize thoroughly the bidders’ documents. We saw that they have the requisite performance security posted. We failed to notify that the amount is inadequate. We advised the winning bidder to increase the requisite amount to conform with law. Rest assured that in the succeeding projects, the BAC will be stricter in the scrutiny of documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the members of the BAC submit justification/ explanation on the noted violations and ensure that prospective bidders submit complete eligibility requirements and post the necessary bid security before they are marked as “passed” and “post-audited”.
observations.managementAction: The BAC admitted lapses in judgement when it awarded the project as noted by COA that there is a failure of the contractor to show proof of lease, ownership or certificate of availability of the Isuzu Forward Dump Truck. The management have already advised the contractor to provide the same to BAC and will transmit to COA office once available

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC strictly complies with the procedures on the receipt, opening and preliminary examination of bids as provided for in the Procurement Manual for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The management have already complied with the COA recommendation. To explain the management side regarding the matter, there were times when access to PhilGEPS website weren’t possible and even went to Manila to ask assistance on the access issue.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the members of the BAC submit justification/explanation on the noted violations and ensure that winning bidders submit complete documentary requirements prior to entering into contract.
observations.managementAction: The project was already awarded to Robustek when their president Rodolfo Abdon died. We feared delay might occur if the project will not proceed, so BAC allowed Robustek to submit its 2017 FS. Robustek promised to fixed their FS pending the election of a new president. The contractor did comply and have already submitted their FS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC strictly complies with the procedures on the receipt, opening and preliminary examination of bids as provided for in the Procurement Manual for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The management have already complied with the COA recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management justifies the above-noted lapses.
observations.managementAction: Many of the beneficiaries are illiterate and although literate, they do not have consistent set of signatures. As such, they have to create their own signature on the spot. Because of this, their signatures sometimes may look different. The management visited some of the beneficiaries and they admitted they have already forgotten their signatures when they signed the documents. Management proposes that the beneficiaries with consistent signatures will be required to affix their thumb marks instead. The subsequent attachment of their signature will cure any defect in the documents and will erase doubts relating thereto, since the beneficiaries who affixes their signatures will be afforded proper explanation regarding the same.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management comply with the provisions of RA 10121 on the creation of plantilla positions for LDRRM Assistants in order to achieve the standard manpower requirements of the DRRM for the efficient and effective delivery of services to its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The DRRM Assistants were created but these positions were unfunded due to lack of funds. Management however, designated Mr. Paul Claor and Mr Rasdy V. Bautista, both regular employees of the Municipality as DRRM Assistants through office Memorandum no. 126, series of 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that,
henceforth, Management adhere
strictly with the provisions of
the Revised IRR of RA No.
9184, particularly on the
issuance of certification on the
conduct of detailed engineering
surveys and designs and posting
of the NOA in the PhilGEPS
and in any conspicuous place
reserved for the purpose. We
recommended further that the
BAC always indicate the date of
approval by the HOPE of the
BAC Resolution declaring and
recommending the award to the
bidder, as well as the date of
issuance of the NOA and
receipt thereof by the winning
bidder in order to establish the
propriety and regularity of the
procurement activities.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 AAR Management strictly observed the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in the implementation of subsequent projects. A personnel was assigned to review the documents and the posting requirements of NOA.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local
Chief Executive enter into
contract with the winning bidder
within ten days from receipt by
the winning bidder of the NOA
in accordance with Section
37.2.2 of the Revised IRR of RA
No. 9184, to avoid delay in the
implementation of projects. We
also recommended that the date
when the contract is executed
should always be indicated in the
contract agreement.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 AAR The Local Chief Executive carefully observed the allowable timelines in the implementation of projects. Contracts and supporting documents were reviewed to ensure completeness of necessary data/information.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management issued the NTP to the winning bidder within seven
calendar days from the date of
approval of the contract in
accordance with Section 37.4.1 of
the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 AAR The Municipal Mayor saw to it that the required timeline in the procurement process is complied with.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and
Management agreed to ensure
timely completion of projects by
directing the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) to observe the
provisions of the IRR particularly
on post-qualification of
contractors to determine the
capability of the contractors to
implement several projects at the
same time. We further
recommended that the Municipal
Engineer monitor closely the
implementation of projects in
accordance with the terms and
conditions of the contract.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 AAR The Municipal Mayor instructed the BAC to conduct a thorough eligibility checking and post-qualification of contractors to undertake projects. The Municipal Engineer and the MPDC conducted regular monitoring of projects to ensure timely completion. Contractor’s attention had been called in case on delays noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management require the
contractor to post additional bond
to comply with the amount
prescribed in Section 62.2.3.3 of
the Revised IRR of RA 9184 to
protect the interest of the
government.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 AAR The Municipal Accountant required the contractor additional bond on projects which are within the one-year liability period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the
Municipal Mayor require the
BAC to prepare the PMR and
submit the same to the GPPB in
printed and electronic format
within 14 calendar days after the
end of each semester in
accordance with Section 12.2 of
the Revised IRRs of R.A. 9184.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 AAR A personnel was assigned to assist the BAC in the preparation of the semestral PMRs for submission to GPPB .

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management direct the personnel
concerned to submit the
documentary requirements for
the payment of security service
contracts and install monitoring
controls to ensure reliability of
actual service reported, such as:
a. Require the Security Guard on
duty to punch upon arrival and
after Duty;
b. Maintain an attendance logbook
and require the Security Guard to
log his time in and out;
c. Assign personnel to check the
logbook on a daily basis;
d. Include in the
Acceptance/Inspection Report
data from the attendance logbook
such as the number of guards on
duty for the period; and
e. Other additional control
measures that management may
deem necessary.
observations.managementAction: CY 2018 AAR The concerned Security Guard submitted the Daily Time Record (DTR) to support the payment for services rendered. A logbook was provided to log the time of arrival and departure as basis for the entries made in the DTR.

observations.recommendation:
Initiate concrete steps to comply
with the minimum considerations
for a dumpsite as provided under
Section 39 of R.A. 9003. We
further recommend that
Management request the
members of the Sangguniang
Bayan to allocate funds for the
establishment of a sanitary
landfill in compliance with
Section 40 and 41 of the same
Republic Act.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
 The General Services Officer conduct regular reconciliation of RPCPPE with the accounting records to fully identify the discrepancies noted. With regard to disposal, complete the necessary documents and perform the subsequent procedures in accordance with the Circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are fully supported with Property Cards and updated Property Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment; and
 The Accounting Department continue all the efforts to secure the necessary documents to fully identify the PPEs included in the beginning/unidentified balances to be able to reconcile with the Inventory Report of the GSO. The Municipality may also refer to COA Circular No. 2020-006, dated January 31, 2020 for the procedures in derecognition of PPEs with no available records of accountability; and
 The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and verified.

This Office is continuously exerting effort to go over available records/ subsidiary ledgers to address the deficiency noted in the audit observations. Moreover, this Office has referred to the COA Circular No. 2020-006, dated January 31, 2020 and has already coordinated with concerned officials for proper planning.
In the meanwhile, this Office provides disclosures of PPEs with beginning/ unidentified balances in the Notes to Financial Statements.
Please be respectfully informed that the Office of the Municipal Engineer will closely assist and cooperate with the Accounting Office and the General Services Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of all Property, Plant and Equipment (PPEs) particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structure Accounts and will help ensure that all PPEs in those accounts are counted and verified.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:

Make use of the template provided in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008 or Appendix 63 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and complete the necessary information in every field. Submit the accomplished RPCLRN to the Accounting Office for reconciliation in the books;
 The Municipal Engineer provide cost segregation of every road networks owned by the Municipality per road component for the prior years. Submit the road networks duly segregated per road component to the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal GSO; and
 The Municipal Accountant provide strategies to identify the breakdown of beginning balances of Road networks. As to the identified Road Network projects, coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to provide individual cost per road component to be the basis in the computation of depreciation expenses.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accounting Office acknowledges the lapses (incomplete accounting and reporting/ inaccuracy in computation of depreciation) relative to Local Road Network System of the LGU as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008. However, Local Road Network Ledger Cards (LRNLCs) for Road Networks acquired in CYs 2018-2020 were duly filled out by this Office from the cost segregation of every road networks owned by the Municipality per road component provided by the Office of the Municipal Engineer.
Adjustments in depreciation of these identified road components were already effected in the books as of March 31, 2021. Complete report on Local Road Network with segregated component for Road Networks acquired in CY 2017 and prior years was not yet provided to this Office by the Municipal Engineering Office, thus these Local Road Networks are still presented in lump-sum figures.
As to identification of the breakdown of beginning balances of Road Networks, this Office is continuously exerting effort to go over available records/ subsidiary ledgers to address the deficiency noted in the audit observations.
In the meanwhile, this Office provides disclosures of PPEs with beginning/ unidentified balances in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.reason: Complete report on Local Road Network with segregated component for Road Networks acquired in CY 2017 and prior years was not yet provided to this Office by the Municipal Engineering Office, thus these Local Road Networks are still presented in lump-sum figures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant consider the above mentioned observations/deficiencies in the preparation of the Notes to Financial Statements Reconciliation of Budget Actual Amounts to Actual Amounts in the Statement of Financial Performance and comply with the stated objective of providing additional information to the intended users of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
Share from Internal Revenue Collections (IRA) presented in the Statement of Financial Performance and Statement of Cash Flows amounting to ₱ 287,574,074.00 and ₱ 230,059,259.20, respectively, shows a total difference of ₱ 57,514,814.80 or equivalent to 20% of the total Share from IRA in CY 2020. This discrepancy is due to the accounting entries made by this Office to record in the books the receipt of Share from IRA.
Interest Income presented in the Statement of Financial Performance and Statement of Cash Flows amounting to ₱ 211,076.15 and ₱ 173,902.73, respectively, shows a total difference of ₱ 37,173.42. This discrepancy relates to the Interest Income earned by Trust Fund in CY 2020. Said income account was erroneously recorded in the Other Receipts Section of Cash Flows from Operating Activities in the Statement of Cash Flows under Trust Fund Books. This inconsistency in the book of Trust Fund has already been corrected by this Office. Statement of Cash Flows as of March 31, 2021 under Trust Fund Books
Appropriations for capital outlays include Repairs/ Maintenance/ Rehabilitation/ Improvement of Public Infrastructures, Buildings and Other Structures, and yet presented in the capital outlay section of the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts. Under the recognition principle, an LGU shall not recognize in the carrying amount of an item of PPE the costs of the day-to-day servicing of the item. Rather, these costs are recognized in surplus or deficit as incurred. Costs of day-to-day servicing are primarily the costs of labor and consumables, and may include the cost of small parts. The purpose of these expenditures is often described as for the “repairs and maintenance” of the item of PPE.
Following the preceding paragraph, this Office, is recording, classifying and presenting expenditures which are operating expenses in nature, although appropriated as capital outlay, under the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses in the Statement of Financial Performance. Thus, these expenditures were presented as reconciling item under the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses Section in the Reconciliation between Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) and in the Statement of Financial Performance. However, in the next SCBAA preparation, said reconciling item will no longer be presented in the capital expenditures but in separate line item under the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses Section of the SCBAA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with the provisions of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 of the Department of Education, Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local Government and prepare the annual and supplemental budget as prescribed for a more transparent and accountable reporting. We further recommended that the Board effectively utilize the Fund with the objective of providing quality elementary and secondary education in the Municipality and abide with the provisions of procurement Act that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) shall only recommend the award of the contract and not award it to the qualified contractor or supplier.
observations.managementAction:
It was noted in the Audit Observation that SEF - LSB Budget was not properly presented in the prescribed format, and the undersigned humbly asks for reconsideration. Notably, ranking will be highly considered so that the top priorities of every school will be strictly followed. After reading all your recommendations DepEd San Juan with all LSB Members shall adhere and abide Section 12 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 with regards to awarding of contracts.
Likewise, it is worthy to mention that the largest amount from the SEF was allotted to the construction of school buildings/classrooms of Pulangbato, Libato National High Schools and Bataan Elementary School amounting to the total of two million eight hundred twenty thousand pesos (₱2 820 000.00).
However, due to pandemic period the SEF budget for CY 2020 was not fully utilized and implemented and the construction of three school buildings, specifically Libato, Pulangbato NHS and Bataan Elementary School were delayed. The spread of COVID-19 has disrupted contractors and supplies of goods and materials. It is due to the fact that LGU San Juan prioritized infrastructure projects such as Concreting of Barangay Roads and Flood Control Projects. since there was no face to face classes due to pandemic, program of works for the construction of above-mentioned school buildings was made on the fourth quarter of the year 2020 and the bidding took place last March 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes. We also recommended that the development programs/projects/activities shall be planned in itemized appropriations in the municipal budget and in the municipal annual investment program as well as in its Local Development Investment Program specifying therein projects proposed for inclusion in the local government budget and any changes/reversions/realignments shall be made pursuant to Articles 417 and 418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction:
AOM 2020-004 (2019) was received by the LGU on June 11, 2020, hence the recommendations given by your Office was not taken into account in AIP and Budget preparation for FY 2020, since those documents were prepared during the last quarter of 2019. However, in 2020 preparation for FY 2021 Budget, project, programs and activities for 20% DF was itemized already and provided relevant information and data as to identified barangay beneficiaries of the project in conformity to Articles 410 and 454(d) of the IRR of RA 7160.
The realignment of current legislative appropriations and enactment of Appropriation Ordinance No. 05, series of 2020 for Supplemental Budget No. 03, series of 2020 amounting to P3,232,523.63 intended for various development projects were approved in March 2020, three months before the LGU received AOM 2020-02 (2019), hence, the recommendation given by your Office was not given due consideration. Since then, however, the LGU adheres to your recommendations, specifically the provisions of Articles 417 and 418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer and her personnel to intensify their collecting power, and strictly impose the provisions of the Municipal Revenue Code and the terms and conditions of the Lease Contracts particularly in the imposition of penalty for the delay or non-payment of market rental fees and advise the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to furnish the Municipal Treasury Office with the complete copies of all the contracts of market stall holders to serve as basis in the increase of monthly rentals to be collected.

We have further recommended that management coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan Members to revisit the Revised Revenue Code for possible revision and modification of some terms and conditions in the collection of market stall rental fees particularly on penalties/surcharges for late payments/non-payments of rentals so that stallholders will be compelled to pay their obligations promptly.

Licenses, Mayor’s permits and contracts were renewed for businesses located in the public market despite non-payment of rentals.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to be strict in renewing the Lease Contracts of the stallholders by securing a clearance or certification from the Municipal Treasurer on the payments made by each stallholder. Coordination be observed between the offices concerned to avoid similar instances in the future.

We have also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer advise the Permits and Licensing Unit under her Office to closely monitor the payments of the market stall owners before renewing their license and permits and see to it that the business index cards are regularly updated.

Finally, the Municipal Government be more responsible in the administration and management of the public market operations in order to make the public market more responsive and effective instrument of public service, as well as dynamic and viable enterprise to strengthen its economic potential, with the ultimate goal of contributing significantly to local and national development.
