1.9 We recommended for Management to:

a. require the reconciliation of property and accounting records on
every PPE account classification and effect the necessary
adjustments, if any;
b. require the Property Unit to maintain complete, accurate and up-
dated Property Cards (PCs) and reconcile the same regularly with
the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELCs)
maintained by the Accounting Office;
c. require the property custodian to file the PARs/AREs of all
movable non- expendable properties and cause the renewal of the
same every January of the 3rd year after issue in order to facilitate
the physical count of PPE and to easily identify the
official/employee responsible in case of loss, resignation, or
expiration of term of office;
d. require the posting of property tag/number on the prominent
portion of every movable non expendable property/equipment,
and
e. proper disposal of unserviceable properties be made.

2.9 We recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the
Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No.
2015-008 dated Nov. 23 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the
Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road
components for every road network. Likewise, require the General
Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and
keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its
components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the
Physical Count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the
Road Network System.

3.4 We recommended that Management, thru the Accounting Office
reclassifies the Market account to Investment Property, Buildings
account to conform with IPSAS 16-Investment Property, for fair
presentation of financial statements.

4.4 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the
Municipal Assessor prepares and furnishes the Municipal Accountant a
duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due
and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card or computer
generated report to provide an accurate and reliable basis in the setting-
up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at
the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the
financial statements.

5.5 We recommended Management to formulate additional strategies to
intensify and facilitate tax collections. If needed, avail of remedies
embodied in Section 174 of RA 7160 to ensure collection of taxes due the
municipal government in order to increase the collection efficiency.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepares the
Certified List of All Property Tax Delinquencies to include all tax
delinquents and reconcile the same with the accounting records.

6.3 We recommended Management to prepare and submit Activity
Design/Training Program to indicate details of the activity undertaken.
Similarly, Minutes of meetings, agendas/programs and complete list of
recipients/attendance with their signatures should be attached as an
addition to establish reasonableness of the claims.

7.7 In view of the above observations, we recommended the following:

 the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer trace
back the source of the discrepancies of the Cash in Bank account
balances and record all book reconciling items to bring the
balances into agreement so that the correct balance of cash is
presented in the financial statements;
 the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer verify
from the bank the details of all debit and credit memos reflected in
the bank statements to record correctly these transactions; and
 verified reconciling items should be taken up immediately after
the preparation of the monthly reconciliation statements.

9.4 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer instructs the collectors
and all other local accountable officers to maintain and use cashbooks for
proper monitoring of collections and remittances, thus, precluding
possible mishandling and misappropriation of collections pursuant to the
requirements of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

10.5 We recommended that Management grant cash advances only to
employees who are properly bonded and duly authorized/designated as
Disbursing Officer pursuant to Section 4.1.5 of COA Circular No. 97-002
and Section 305(f) of RA 7160.

12.7 We recommended that the local government unit require concerned
officials and employees to submit within reglementary period the monthly
accounts covering receipts and collections and financial reports on time in
compliance with the above-cited provisions/laws/regulations.

13.5 We reiterated our recommendation that Management submit perfected
copy of the government contracts to COA within five working days from
perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular 2009-001 to enable timely
auditorial and technical review.

14.3 We recommended that the Municipal Government stops the practice of
allowing job orders to claim per diems. When these employees are
directed to perform tasks that require them to travel, they should be
allowed to claim actual fare only.

15.5 We recommended the Local Government Unit Personnel Officer to
require all employees concerned to submit properly filled up daily time
records with complete attachments as indicated in the daily time records
submitted.

16.4 We recommended that Management justifies why the prize received was
not deposited intact and why was the succeeding deposit was made only
after ten months.

17.4 We recommended that the Municipality establish specific guidelines in
the grant of financial assistance to the indigents. The Audit Team
suggested that said guidelines be patterned on the DSWD Administrative
Order No. 5, series of 2008 otherwise known as the Omnibus Guidelines
on the Management of DSWD Operated Crisis Intervention Unit as
amended by DSWD Administrative Order No. 1 series of 2011.

18.3 We recommended that Management justifies the above noted lapses and
ensures compliance with the minimum standards on disaster
preparedness specifically on service delivery to guarantee that support is
established during emergencies.
20.5 We recommended that Management to justifies the noted lapses.

21.6 We recommended that the members of the BAC submit
justification/explanation on the noted violations and ensure that
prospective bidders submit complete eligibility requirements and post the
necessary bid security before they are marked as “passed” and “post-
audited”.

23.3 We recommended that the Municipal Government, through the BAC
Secretariat complies with the posting requirements on all its
procurement. Notices of Award, Notice to Proceed, and the approved
contract be posted in the PhilGEPS website and website of the
municipality, if any, within the reglementary period as required by the
Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

24.3 We recommended that the BAC strictly complies with the procedures on
the receipt, opening and preliminary examination of bids as provided for
in the Procurement Manual for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A.
No. 9184.
25.6 We recommended that Management justifies the above noted lapses.

26.3 We recommended that Management complies with the provisions of RA
10121 on the creation of plantilla positions for LDRRM Assistants in
order to achieve the standard manpower requirements of the DRRM
Office for the efficient and effective delivery of services to its constituents.

27.5 We recommended that Management justifies the above-noted lapses and
limit the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to programs, projects
and activities that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic
development and environmental management outcomes and ensure that
the same partakes the nature of investment or capital expenditures that
will yield greater benefit to the people in accordance with DBM-DILG
JMC No. 2017-1.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management instruct:
The CAD to -
Follow-up the request of dropping off from the books of the PPE items which are lumped as beginning balances and secure the approval of competent authority; and
Reconcile the amounts of the PPE recorded in the books with the RPCPPE prepared by the Inventory Committee before incorporating the balances of the PPE accounts in the financial statements. If there are differences found between the two records, strict scrutiny should be made especially those material in amounts before making the necessary adjustments;
The GSD to -
Conduct a complete actual physical count of PPE, not only those under GF, but also under SEF, and submit the corresponding RPCPPE to COA not later than January 31 of each year; and
Coordinate with the CAD for the updating of records to arrive at the reconciled balances between the books of accounts and the RPCPPE.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management instruct the General Services Department to:
Prepare and submit an updated Property Insurance Form to the GSIS in order to arrive at the correct assessment of all the risks attached to the insurable properties of the City by the GSIS Insurance Group using the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-390 dated November 17, 1992; and
Insure all the insurable properties of the City as prescribed by RA 656, COA Circular Nos. 92-390 and 2018-002.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The City Assessor’s Office to –
Ascertain whether all real properties within the jurisdiction of the City are subjected to equitable or indiscriminate revision of real property assessment, especially the noted 982 buildings and 643 machineries pursuant to the provisions of City Ordinance No. 46, Series of 2017;
Compute the taxes due from the date of effectivity of the general revision; and
The City’s Board of Assessment, in coordination with the SP, to develop policies/guidelines that when a machinery is no longer used for its purpose by reason of closure or cessation of business, the same should be transferred from the Taxable Roll to the Exempt Roll.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The CTD to –
Provide a complete list of delinquent taxpayers to avoid understatement of the balances of RPT and SET accounts;
Assign personnel to undertake regular updating of the list of delinquent taxpayers to avoid erroneous/incorrect information relative thereto; and
The CAO to properly tag RPUs in the RPTAS-Assessment Roll after all the necessary documentary requirements are submitted by the real property owners seeking tax exemption.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The Business Permits and Licensing Office to revisit/review the BPLTAS relative to the existing embedded business tax formula;  asertain the proper categories of all business establishments; and facilitate necessary corrections in the system; and
The City Treasury Department to notify all business owners with underpayment of taxes noted in the audit and expedite the necessary demand for payment, otherwise, Notices of Charge for the noted deficiencies will be issued.

We recommended that Management utilize the 20% DF within the scheduled period of implementation as indicated in the Annual Investment Program so as not to deprive the constituents  of the benefits which could be derived thru the timely implementation of the funded projects, and to ensure efficient outputs that would improve the socio-economic condition of the City.
We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF for the programs/projects/activities pursuant to the City’s disaster risk management plan.

We recommended that Management instruct the CAD to:
Record the goods purchased out of the LDRRMF in the appropriate inventory account and record the issuances as they take place in the proper expense account; and
Regularly coordinate with the GSD relative to the issued items to be recorded as expenses.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Include the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the LDRRMFIP in compliance with Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-02; and
Submit to COA the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on a monthly basis and course it through the LDRRMC and LDC using the format provided in the same COA Circular.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the City’s GFPS to prepare quarterly/semestral monitoring reports showing the status of the implementation of the planned PPAs as well as the reasons for the delayed and/or non-implementation of the said PPAs to ensure the timely and complete utilization of the GAD budget.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Planning and Development Officer (CPDO) to prioritize the establishment of GAD database and exhaust all available resources for the collection and processing of its data requirements.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation and Management agreed to:
Review the GAD Code as to its relevance to current gender issues and concerns in the locality based on updated consultation with women constituents in accordance with the MCW and recommend amendments to improve/update its provisions; and
Promulgate the IRR on the City GAD Code of 2017 in accordance with Section 28 of the City Ordinance No. 82, series of 2017, to ensure the effective implementation of the said Ordinance.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Establish the City’s M&E Team defining the names and duties/responsibilities of the members; and
Submit the required GAD Evaluation Report, and annual status report to the DILG Regional Office pursuant to the abovementioned JMC.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to deduct the amount of P5,575,680 from the succeeding claims of the contractor for the said project to avoid issuance of a notice of disallowance in post-audit.

We recommended that Management require the CED to:
Submit an explanation on the deficiencies noted for the unnecessary materials used on the subject projects; and
Carefully review the plans/specifications as well as the scope of works before processing the request for the procurement of materials for the repair of the City’s assets.

We recommended that Management instruct the CED to:
Inform COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted;
Submit at the beginning of every year the required list of all on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented for the year; and
Submit the monthly monitoring report on the status of the said PPAs.

We recommended that Management:
Undertake assessment and evaluation of the viability of the operation of these economic enterprises and decide whether to establish separate books of accounts for each LEE, or maintain special accounts only for every LEE in the GF proper; and
Cause the in-house development of the existing accounting system to incorporate the revision for the accounts pertaining to the respective LEEs.

We recommended that the concerned officials/employees strictly comply with the rules and regulations governing the proper control and usage of government motor vehicles, particularly in the preparation and submission of the duly accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets, Monthly Report of Official Travels (MROT) and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption (MRFC).

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to:
Prepare monthly schedule supporting the Due to BIR account to monitor the amounts withheld and remitted and to determine the cause of the noted abnormal balances; and
Remit the exact amount of monthly withheld taxes to the BIR to avoid payment of penalties and the punishment under Item VII-B of Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014; and
Cause the necessary correction for the abovementioned error in the GAAMS as well as the discrepancy amounting to P13,590.78 on the beginning balance of the SL under the SEF.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to:
Remit the full amount of contributions withheld from the salaries of the City’s personnel on the month following the month in which withholding was made to avoid any variances, and hold the person/s responsible for the delayed remittances to personally answer for the penalties and surcharges that may be imposed by the concerned collecting agency; and
Recheck/review the accuracy of the total amount of contributions and the amount to be remitted to concerned agencies every month before making any remittances to avoid under and/or over payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the
required monthly BRS to the Audit Team within ten (10) days from receipt of the
bank statements after the end of each month in accordance with the existing
regulations.

We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to
conduct an annual physical count of properties and report the results of the count
on the RCPPE to be submitted to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each
year, and reconcile the same with the accounting records. We further
recommended that the Property Custodian – Designee maintain property cards for
allproperties owned bythe Municipality.

We recommended that the MPDC and Municipal Engineer adequately and
thoroughly plan all projects prior to inclusion in the AIP to ensure that only those,
which can be implemented immediately or at least within two years, taking also into
considerationthe project costs, areincluded therein.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council specifically
describe the projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan and always secure
the approval or authorization of the SB before incurring expenditures and/or
entering into contract for the implementation of projects described in appropriation
ordinancein generic terms.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management adhere strictly with the
provisions of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, particularly on the issuance of
certification on the conduct of detailed engineering surveys and designs and posting
of the NOA in the PhilGEPS and in any conspicuous place reserved for the purpose.
We recommended further that the BAC always indicate the date of approval by the
HOPE of the BAC Resolution declaring and recommending the award to the bidder,
as well as the date of issuance of the NOA and receipt thereof by the winning bidder
inorder to establishthe propriety andregularity oftheprocurementactivities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enter into contract with the
winning bidder within ten calendar days from receipt by the winning bidder of the
NOA in accordance with Section 37.2.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, to avoid
delay in the implementation of projects. We also recommended that the date when
thecontract is executed should always beindicated inthe contract agreement.

We recommended that Management issue the NTP to the winning bidder
within seven calendar days from the date of approval of the contract in accordance
with Section 37.4.1oftheRevised IRRofRA No.9184.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure timely completion of
projects by directing the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to observe the
provisions of the IRR particularly on post-qualification of contractors to determine
the capability of the contractors to implement several projects at the same time. We
further recommended that the Municipal Engineer monitor closely the
implementation of projects in accordance with the terms and conditions of the
contract.

We recommended that Management require the contractor to post
additional bond to comply with the amount prescribed in Section 62.2.3.3 of the
Revised IRRof RA9184to protect theinterest of thegovernment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to prepare the
PMR and submit the same to the GPPB in printed and electronic format within 14
calendar days after the end of each semester in accordance with Section 12.2 of the
Revised IRRs of R.A.9184.

We recommended and the MDRRMO agreed to coordinate with the
MDRRMC in identifying the PPAs to be charged to the unexpended balance of the
LDRRMF in the previous years for inclusion in the LDRRMFIP in accordance with
Section 5.1.2ofCOACircular No. 2012-002dated September 12,2012.

We recommended that Management (a) prioritize the acquisition of a
biometric machine or bundy clock to record the attendance of all personnel;
(b) require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that payment of salaries and
RATA are supported with DTRs as proofs of actual service rendered and approved
applications for leaves of absence; and (c) require the Human Resource
Management Officer to monitor the attendance of officials and employees by
maintaining an attendance logbook to record their actual arrival to and departure
fromoffice.

We recommended that Management direct the personnel concerned to
submit the documentary requirements for the payment of security service contracts
and install monitoring controls to ensure reliability of actual service reported, such
as:
a) Require the Security Guard on duty to punch uponarrival and after
Duty;
b) Maintain an attendance logbookand requirethe Security Guard to log
his time in andout;
c) Assignpersonnel tocheck the logbookonadaily basis;
d) Include inthe Acceptance/Inspection Report data from theattendance
logbooksuch as thenumber ofguards onduty forthe period;and
e) Other additional control measures that management may deem
necessary.

We recommended and Management agreed that  the reconciliation of the results of the count with the property and accounting records be given priority by the Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant. Likewise, we recommended that the Inventory Committee ensure prompt submission of duly and completely accomplished RPCPPE.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make a compilation of any record/documentation retrieved from their ongoing efforts and prepare the corresponding assessment/evaluation report as regards the long outstanding liabilities to support the possible reversion.  Subsidiary ledgers and all available records to support the amount should be attached to the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) that has to be prepared in case of reversion to the unappropriated surplus. In the event that there may be claims in the future, the JEV would serve as the basis in requesting for a supplemental budget for the settlement of the payable.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal Assessor and officials concerned to prioritize the transfer of ownership over these parcels of land acquired by the Municipality to avoid exposing the same to possible third party claims and land disputes.

We recommended and Management agreed to religiously remit withheld contributions and loan amortizations on time and evaluate and determine whose account the balance of the Due to GSIS account belongs to in order not to affect the benefits of employees who will opt to retire in the future.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer agreed to give preferential attention to the retrieval of records and determination of the intended purpose of the transferred funds for possibilities of implementing the projects/activities. In cases that the purposes were already accomplished, we recommended that Management immediately remit the balances  to the Bureau of Treasury or  return the unutilized funds to the source agencies to finally end any obligation created by these transfers and to the fully liquidate these accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to strictly observe the timely submission of accounts to facilitate review thereon and avoid the imposition of sanctions as provided under Section 39 (1) (3) of Presidential Decree No. 1445. Likewise, we recommended that Management impose internal rules in the movement or flow of transactions in order to expedite submission of reports and documents and assign additional competent support personnel to the Office of the Municipal Accountant as deemed necessary.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to see to it that Disbursement Vouchers are duly supported with complete documentation in compliance with Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 in order to substantiate the legality, validity and propriety of transactions and in order to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension. Likewise, it is recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit a written explanation why blank Official Receipts were given by the suppliers. We recommended further that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the immediate submission of the lacking supporting documents for audit purposes.

We recommended and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) agreed to carefully examine the transactions of the Municipality in order to correctly recommend to the Head of Procuring Entity the alternative mode of procurement to be applied in compliance with the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) agreed to carefully examine the procurements of goods or services of the Municipality in order to consolidate those with similar nature to avoid splitting of contracts which is strictly prohibited in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and to ensure that the most advantageous price for the Government is obtained.

We recommended and the Secretariat, as a support staff of the Bids and Awards Committee, agreed to see to it that the Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed are posted in the PhilGEPS website within the prescribed period of 3 and 15 calendar days in order to ensure procurement transparency.

We recommended and the BAC agreed to require the winning bidders/contractors to submit duly approved Construction Safety and Health Program as part of the perfected contracts. We also recommended that pending the approval by the DOLE of the program on the date of submission of perfected contracts, Management require a certification that the program has been submitted for approval by the DOLE.

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that upon the final payment of contracts, he will require attachment of duly approved program to ensure compliance with R.A. 9184 and relevant issuances of the DOLE. Likewise, it is recommended that Management require the refund of the cost for the implementation of CSHP amounting to ₱988,800.28 by the concerned contractors.

We recommended and Management agreed, thru the Sangguniang Bayan, to formulate standard implementing guidelines to regulate the financial aid or assistance granted to various individuals in crisis situation or AICS to avoid inequitable allocation to the constituents of the Municipality. We encourage Management to consider the Memorandum Circular No. 11, Series of 2019 issued by the DSWD which sets forth the revised guidelines on the Implementation of AICS.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to cause the immediate implementation of the funded projects out of the share of the Municipality from the tobacco excise taxes through maximum use of all available resources so that benefits will be timely delivered to the tobacco farmers/constituents.

We recommended and Management agreed that they will intensify the collection of real property taxes. Likewise, the acting Municipal treasurer should issue notice of delinquency as required in R.A. 7160 to all delinquent taxpayers for them to settle all their taxes due to augment income of the Municipality that could be used to finance priority programs and projects.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure the full implementation of the priority projects which remained unimplemented in 2019 and ensure that 20% Development Funds be optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU in accordance with the DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure that programs/projects/activities funded with appropriations for Solid Waste Management of the Municipality are limited to those that will contribute to the attainment of the purpose for which it was established. This is to ensure that the Municipality will able to perform their mandated duty under RA 9003 or the Solid Waste Management Act of 2000. The services of the Job Orders, if necessary, should be compensated with appropriations from other source or program of the Municipality.

We have recommended that in order to validate the accuracy, existence and completeness of the properties of the municipality as well as to establish the correct valuation, sufficient control and accountability for each property, the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the following:
The members of the Inventory Committee conduct the annual physical inventory, prepare the RPCPPE and/or may seek assistance or coordinate with the department heads to make inventory of municipal properties in their respective offices, so as to address the manpower constraint in the conduct of physical count.
The Property Officer/GSO and Municipal Accountant update the property/supply ledger cards and subsidiary ledgers, respectively, reconcile the same, and make the necessary adjustments. Additional staff to address the concern on manpower constraints may also be considered.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of infrastructure assets.
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer-Designate with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
The Property Officer-Designate and the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the required records and reports under COA Circular No. 2015-008 for the fair presentation of the Local Road Network account in the financial statements.

We have recommended Management to:
Cause the Municipal Treasurer to update and maintain the RPTARs as primary basis of duly certified list of taxpayers for the corresponding amount of tax collectible as a basis for setting up the RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year. Also, verify and ascertain the list of delinquent taxpayers to its actual collectability; and
Cause the Municipal Accountant to only consider the amount of duly certified list from the Treasury office which reconciles with the amount of collectibles based from the assessment roll of the Assessor’s Office. Make necessary adjustment, if any, to present fairly the financial statements.

In view of the circumstances, we have recommended the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with IPSAS 12 and Section 121 on the Manual of NGAs for LGUs on the proper valuation of inventory and correct recognition of expenses;
Direct the GSO/Property Officer and Municipal Accountant to maintain the required stock cards and supply inventory cards, respectively; and
Strengthen the function and responsibilities of the Physical Inventory Committee by requiring not only to conduct physical count of all inventories but also to submit the RPCI to the Accounting Office for reconciliation with the reported inventory balances per books and to the Office of the Auditor at least once a year, if the semestral reporting is not feasible.

We have recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accounting Office review, confirm and verify all information available to establish the validity and existence of these dormant payables and adjust the accounts accordingly in line with the objective of fair presentation of liabilities in the financial statements.
We have further recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office maintain complete and reliable subsidiary ledgers and conduct regular monitoring of outstanding liabilities.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to make a representation to the Provincial Treasurer’s Office and seek assistance in the enforcement of collection of delinquent taxes, especially those long over-due, for additional funds of the Municipality. Moreover, to ask assistance from the Barangay Officials to inform the heirs of the deceased delinquent taxpayers or occupants of the properties to pay their delinquent taxes.

We have recommended that the Management:
Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by speeding up the implementation of the prioritized programs, projects and activities as per Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and that closer coordination among officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring be undertaken to achieve the desired level of outcomes.
Instruct the Local Development Council to immediately look into the unimplemented developmental projects as embodied in the APP and develop feasible and specific solutions to effectively implement those projects.
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to be very prudent, meticulous, conscientious and precise in evaluating the timely and perfect implementation of each and every project. Likewise, conduct regular inspection of projects together with the Project Monitoring Committee so that accomplishment is monitored and evaluated based on the project implementation schedule as stipulated in the APP.
Ensure that priority socio-economic and development programs and projects are executed within the fiscal year to facilitate delivery of public services at the soonest possible time.

We have recommended that management stop from charging PPAs which are not directly related to disaster risk reduction and management activities to the LDRRM Fund and the Municipal Accounting and Budget Offices to strictly comply with the provisions of RA 10121, COA Circular No. 2012-002 and JMC No. 2013-1.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to be strict in renewing the Lease Contracts of the stallholders by securing a clearance or certification from the Municipal Treasurer on the payments made by each stallholder. Coordination be observed between the offices concerned to avoid similar instances in the future.
We have also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer advise the Permits and Licensing Unit under her Office to closely monitor the payments of the market stall owners before renewing their license and permits and see to it that the business index cards are regularly updated.
Finally, the Municipal Government be more responsible in the administration and management of the public market operations in order to make the public market more responsive and effective instrument of public service, as well as dynamic and viable enterprise to strengthen its economic potential, with the ultimate goal of contributing significantly to local and national development.

We have recommended and Management agreed to fast track the implementation of these priority programs to address the environmental concerns on solid waste management and to continue its efforts towards a more effective and efficient arm of the government in conserving natural resources.

We have recommended that Management ensure that Budget and Accounting Office strictly comply with the provision of RA 9679 in observing the maximum limit of ₱100.00 per month per official/employee.
Further, we have also recommended that concerned employees/officials refund the relative amount due to the LGU as these were already applied as part of their provident savings and future claims from the HDMF.

We have recommended that management maximize the utilization of the GAD fund and implement programs/projects/ activities as planned to address concerns on women empowerment and human rights and gender responsive issues.
We have further recommended that management direct the GAD Focal Point System to administer the HGDG tests for the formulation of GAD PPAs that will specifically address the gender gaps/issues and concerns.
Finally, we have recommended that the LCE issue an Executive Order for the establishment of monitoring and evaluation system through the creation of Monitoring and Evaluation Team that will evaluate the results of the Municipality’s GAD policies, programs and projects.

We have recommended that the Management direct the BAC and TWG to:
Strictly scrutinize the sufficiency of the winning bidder’s manpower and equipment on all the projects to be awarded and not on an individual project, only in cases where the latter is the lowest bidder in several projects which have similar or overlapping implementation period. The winning contractor is required to supplement its manpower and/or equipment to be utilized in the projects awarded to it or to disqualify it from obtaining several awards or contracts in conformity with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
Efficiently evaluate during the bid evaluation and post qualification the accuracy of the List of All Ongoing Government and Private Contracts and the NFCC of the bidders who were awarded several projects if they accurately reflect its financial capacity to absorb the additional works and to finance its implementation or completion.
We have recommended further that Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Officer to strictly and closely monitor all projects simultaneously implemented by the contractors to check, verify and evaluate the quality of work and efficiency of the said contractors.

We have reiterated our recommendation that the HoPE direct the BAC to prepare the Project Monitoring Report and approve by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We have reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, as head of the Procuring Entity, ensure that prior to contract signing for infrastructure projects, require the winning bidder to submit CSHP approved by the DOLE which forms part of contract in order to promote the welfare of the contruction workers.

We have recommended that management require the MSWDO and the PHWs to submit the supporting documents such as approved DTR, travel order covering assignment to hazardous/difficult areas or Accomplishment Reports as proof of actual service rendered during the period being claimed and that the same be recomputed in accordance with the provisions in Sec. 16 of the IRR of RA No. 9433 and RA No.7305 and DBM-DOH Joint Circular No.1, s. 2016.

We have recommended that Management:
a. Limit the payroll deductions to the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee’s net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay which is ₱5,000.00 as provided under Section 52 of Republic Act 11620.
b. Ensure that the required minimum take home pay is attained after all the mandatory and other authorized deductions from the salary of each employee are deducted from the gross salary to guarantee that there is enough amount to sustain the needs of the employee for a month.
c. See to it that an employee applying for a loan will only avail of the amount that has a monthly amortization of not more than the amount in excess of ₱5,000.00 after deducting all the authorized deductions.

 The General Services Officer (GSO) conduct regular reconciliation of RPCPPE with the
accounting records to fully identify the discrepancies noted. With regard to
disposal, complete the necessary documents and perform the subsequent
procedures in accordance with the Circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are
fully supported with Property Cards and updated Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment; and
 The Accounting Department continue all the efforts to secure the necessary
documents to fully identify the PPEs included in the beginning/unidentified
balancesto be able to reconcile with the Inventory Report of the GSO,otherwise,
provide disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.Lastly, review the
breakdown of the items per PPE account and ensure that there is consistent
application of accounting policies in recording PPEs. If there is a need for
reclassification, prepare the necessary journal entry.

 The Municipal GSO to prepare the Report on Local Road Network using the
prescribed format in COA Circular No. 2015-008. Likewise, prepare the Road
Network Property Card using the template provided in the circular. Using the
RLRN and the Road Network Property Cards, reconcile with the Municipal
Accounting records the properly report on its Road Network account balances;
 The Municipal Engineer provide cost segregation of every road networks owned
by the Municipality per road component. Submit the road networks duly
segregated per road component to the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
GSO; and
 The Municipal Accountant provide strategies to identify the breakdown of
beginning balances of Road networks. As to the identified Road Network projects,
coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to provide individual cost per road
component to be the basis in the computation of depreciation expenses. Likewise,
ensure that the information in the Road Network Ledger Card are complete or the
ledger card be duly filled out.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
exhaust all efforts and devise strategies to collect all delinquent real property taxes
and encourage the taxpayer to pay their dues on time. Conduct verification on those
outstanding balances to determine the accuracy of the receivables and its
collectability and if warranted enforce the provisions Sections 258 and 269 of RA 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Accountant and her staff
to:
1. Coordinate again with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile their records on
receivables/payables and their corresponding payables/receivables in the books
of the Province to establish the correct balances of the inter-agency accounts.
2. Coordinate with the concerned officials and departments heads to obtain
information on those long outstanding accounts. Secure the necessary
documents to properly identify the creditor/local government unit to whom it’s
due. Once the specific local government unit had been identified, verify the
reported balances in the subsidiary ledgers through confirmation letters and
exhaust efforts to fully ascertain the validity and legality of those obligations
3. Review the composition of the accounts under the Due to LGUs and identify the
appropriate classification for those items identified in the audit. Secure proper
documentation before effecting the necessary adjusting entries to ensure that the
amounts are valid and conform to the rules and regulations that will cover such
adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget
Officer consider the above mentioned observations/deficiencies in the preparation of Notes
to Financial Statements Reconciliation of Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities to
Surplus/(Deficit) and Notes to Financial Statements Reconciliation of Budget Actual
Amounts to Actual Amounts in the Statement of Financial Performanceand comply with
the stated objective of providing additional information to the intended users of the
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
 The Municipal Accountant immediately submit the liquidation reports if not yet
submitted and prepare the check for the source agency to refund the unexpended
balance. See to it that future fund transfers are fully implemented within the time
frame to avoid delay in liquidation.
 The Head of the Municipal Planning and Development Office coordinate with the
concerned department heads to enforce the utilization and implementation of
those projects that are not yet implemented while pursue the continuation of
those ongoing utilization. Likewise, see to it that their respective time frames and
schedules are strictly monitored and evaluated to avoid delays in its
implementation.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with the
provisions of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 of the Department of Education,
Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local
Government in order to ensure that Special Education Fund (SEF) will be utilized for
the intended purpose or according to the identified priority areas listed in the Annual
Implementation Plan (AIP) culled from the DepEd-approved School Improvement Plan
(SIP) and the Annual and Supplemental Budget are presented in adherence to the
said guidelines.

We further recommended that the Board refrain from charging the Fund
expenses not in accordance with what are prescribed in Section 1 of Republic Act No.
5447; Sections 100(c) and 272 of the Local Government Code and DepEd-DBM-DILG
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 s. 2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects
that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes.

We further recommended the strict observance of Section 3.2 of Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1 Series of 2007 dated March 8, 2007 of Department of the
Interior and Local Government, National Economic and Development Authority,
Department of Budget and Management and Department of Finance that the Local
Finance Committee (LFC) and Local Development Council (LDC) ensure that the
Annual Investment Program is the annual slice of the Local Development Investment
Program (LDIP) and adhere to the prescribed format provided in Section 8.6of Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1, series of 2016 of DILG, NEDA, DBM and DOF dated
November 18, 2016 in the preparation of Local Development Investment Program
(LDIP).

 Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office
(MDRRMO) to implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under70%
Mitigation Fundwithin the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of services
and benefits to the constituents.
 The Municipal Accountant provide Subsidiary Ledgers per year of transfer of the
unexpended balance of DRRM Funds that were transferred to the Trust Liability
account to fully identify which of the balances are over five years and for
reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund for appropriation for
other social services.

We recommended that the Municipality pursue gender mainstreaming
completely in the locality. Establish the GAD Database and formulate the GAD Code
to institutionalize the processes/mechanisms in the implementation of the Magna
Carta of Women in the LGU.

We further recommended that attributed program/project be supported with
necessary documents that should have been generated or used in the administration
of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) tools in the evaluation of
its gender responsiveness during design and identification; management and
implementation and monitoring and evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipality reorganize the Municipal Local Council
for the Protection of Children (MLCPC) pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 9344 or
the “Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006” and also activate, strengthen, and
sustain the reorganized MLCPC. Allocate the mandatory budget of one (1) percent of
internal revenue allotment and prioritize in the Council’s activities the preparation of
Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, Local
Investment Plan for Children, Local Code for Children, Local State of Children’s
Report, Children’s Profile and the establishment, maintenance and updating of data
base on children for the strengthening and proper implementation of the programs of
the council.

We recommended that the Municipality fully implement the Municipal Solid
Waste Management Plan covering the period CY 2015 to 2025 pursuant to the
provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 or “Philippine Ecological Solid Waste
Management Act of 2000” that espouses on the implementation of the 3R or the
reduce, reuse and recycle concept with focus on reduction of pollution and waste
generation.

We recommended that the Municipal Local Development Council strictly
observe the provisions of Sections 106 and 109 (a) of RA No. 7160 in the performance
of its conferred functions and the Sangguniang Bayan of that Municipality in the
exercise of its delegated local legislative power considering the provisions of Section
447 (a) (2) (i) of RA No. 7160; Articles 417, 418, and 454 of the Act Implementing Rules
and Regulations and Sections 5.1.11 to 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2016-002.

We recommended that the Municipality particularly the concerned local
government officials strictly observe the provisions of Articles 417 and 418 of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 7160. Local government funds shall
only be available for expenditures pursuant to appropriation ordinance enacted by
the local sanggunian based on the laws, rules and regulations governing its
operations.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that:

The CAD:

observations.managementAction: During the reconciliation, some PPE accounts were reclassified into its proper accounts in the RPCPPE due to misclassification from the previous years. Hence, proper adjusting entries were made to reflect the necessary adjustments in the record of CAD.
observations.reason: Reconciliation of the variances of PPE accounts is still ongoing.

observations.managementAction: The CAD and GSD had started conducting a joint inspection of PPEs to revalidate the items recorded in the books and to check if there are still unrecorded PPEs. Adjustments were taken to correct the recorded items in the CAD for the PPE items which were transferred to various Barangays in compliance with PPSAS.
observations.reason: Some items lack sufficient proof of existence.

observations.managementAction: Coordination with GSD in conducting thorough reconciliation of records is ongoing.
observations.reason: The balances of PPE accounts incorporated in the financial statements are not reconciled with the RPCPPE due to incomplete conduct of physical inventory.

observations.managementAction: The GSD included the property number and description of each machinery in the RPCPPE.

The City Accountant stated that the CAD and GSD had started conducting a joint inspection of PPEs to revalidate the items recorded in the books and to check if there are still unrecorded PPEs. Accordingly, adjustments were made to correct the recorded items in the CAD for the PPE items which were transferred to various Barangays in compliance with PPSAS.
observations.reason: The variance between the records still exists due to incomplete reconciliation by the CAD and GSD.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the General Services Department to:

Prepare and submit to the GSIS an updated Property Inventory Form to arrive at the correct assessment of all the risks attached to the insurable properties of the City by the

observations.reason: Not all insurable properties were insured by the GSIS since the submitted list was incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer and the City Accountant facilitate a regular reconciliation of the Cashbook and the GL balances to ensure the completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions and balances of the cash accounts, and to make adjusting entries, where appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Regular reconciliation of the Cashbook and the GL balances is being conducted by CTD and CAD.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the CTD to:

Determine the monthly estimated cash requirements of the City to establish the estimated cash balance that would be recommended for investments to enable proper utilization of cash resources and maximize the income that could be derived from the said investments; and

Inquire from time to time with other authorized government depository banks that offer higher interests earnings on time deposit investments.
observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management utilize the 20% DF within the scheduled period of implementation as indicated in the Annual Investment Program so as not to deprive the constituents and the City of the benefits which could be derived thru the timely implementation of the funded projects, and to improve the socio-economic condition of the City.

Refund from the General Fund to the Trust Liability-DRRM account the amount of P15.780 million representing the inappropriate utilization of the LDRRMF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the CGFPS to set up and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-disaggregated data or GAD database considering that this will serve as basis in the performance-based and gender-responsive planning and budgeting.

observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:

Inform COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted and submit, at the beginning of every year, the required list of all on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented for the year pursuant to Items 2.1, 3.1 and 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

observations.recommendation:
For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we recommended the following:

That the Inventory Committee complete the inventory taking and submit the corresponding RPCPPE. Henceforth, conduct the required annual physical count of PPEs  every year and submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor concerned not later than  January 31 of the succeeding year.  This procedure is a must in order to know the physical existence of the PPE and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets. Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary adjustments; and to pin point responsibility/accountability/liability for the missing PPEs. Further, the audit team should be invited to observe during the annual physical count.

That the OIC-Municipal Accountant and the GSO/OIC Municipal Treasurer maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE ledger cards, AREs and place of property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPEs and for facility in identifying them.

observations.reason: The submitted RPCPPE does not reconcile with the balance of PPE as appearing in the financial statements.

CDF – ₱363,886.66 & Sen. Loren Legarda – ₱3,525.48.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer require the submission of Daily Time Record certified correct by the employee concerned and approved by his immediate supervisor before paying their salaries and wages. observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Inventory committee conduct physical count of property, plant and equipment annually and submit a report thereon not later than January 31 of each year and the Accounting and Property Office to maintain complete subsidiary records for these properties. observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management refrain from granting cash advances to Municipal officials and employees and other personnel other than for an authorized official travel. observations.recommendation: We have recommended and Management agreed that through Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), all purchases of various supplies and materials be made through the general mode of procurement which is public bidding to do away with the practice of reimbursement. Strict adherence to the provision under COA Circular No. 97-002 regarding the Imprest Fund system in order to have a more efficient and effective internal control is also enjoined.
observations.managementAction: A written response letter dated March 8, 2019 was submitted to the Audit Team on the same date affirming that the recommendation was being observed since they received the Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. 18-018 dated September 14, 2018.

observations.recommendation:

a. Municipal Engineer to notify the contractors about the defects and require them to perform corrective actions to prolong the life of LGU's infrastructure projects;
  
b. The Municipal Engineer to ensure that the billboards displayed for projects will be fabricated and posted properly by the contractor based on COA Circular No. 2013-04 for transparency and to avoid accident; and

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:  a) direct the General Services Office and the Accounting Office to exhaust all effort in the determination of the particulars, whereabouts, and any documents or information to the audit team.  b) create an Inventory Committee (IC) who will conduct thorough physical inventory taking of the municipal properties, preparation and submission of reconciled RPCPPE with accounting records, so that existence, accuracy and validity of all properties under the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts recorded in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and  c) require the Inventory Committee to submit to the Audit Team the updates/development of the reconciliation between the Accounting and Property records every semester for verification and monitoring purposes.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comments:
Inconsistencies in the classification of accounts might arise in cases where a particular activity might be present in both items such as: Construction of Box Culvert may be present in Improvement of Roads, likewise in Installation of Flood Control System; handheld radios when issued/ utilized by Military/ Police Officers are accounted for under Military, Police and Security Equipment while those issued to government officials are recognized as Communication Equipment in the books. Notwithstanding, adjusting/ reclassification entries have been drawn for recognition in the books to provide consistency in the classification and recording of accounts.
The Accounting Department has been closely monitoring procurement of Property, Plant and Equipment of the Agency based on Disbursement Vouchers, likewise, those donated properties based on the documents furnished to this Office. Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards are being maintained for each item of Property except those pertaining to beginning balances, the particulars of which could not be ascertained as of this period primarily due to absence/insufficiency of available records considering that these were forwarded balances in the books for years now.
Although apparently this undertaking of establishing/ identifying these Property, Plant and Equipment will entail sometime to accomplish, this Office shall continuously exert effort to go over available records/ subsidiary ledgers to address this deficiency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary reclassification to the Merchandise Inventory account from Property, Plant and Equipment-Land Investment Property-Building account the cost of the two-door, two storey apartment-style building of the Merchandise Inventory account.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comments:
The Management quoted the following:
“This Office acknowledges the lapses (incomplete accounting and reporting/inaccuracy in computation of depreciation) relative to Local Road Network System of the LGU as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008. Much as we would like to address this deficiency and comply with said circular, indeed, this Office is being hindered/ precluded by the non-availability of complete report on Local Road Network with segregated component that should have been furnished to this Office, in this case, by the Municipal Engineering Office, having been informed of the COA Circular since 2015/2016. Nevertheless, this office has coordinated with the Municipal Engineer relative to this observation and he has committed to do his best to provide this Office with said report on Local Road Network. As to retrieval of available records to identify beginning balances and component of Road Networks, this Office, although the task at hand might take some time to accomplish, likewise affirms to exert our utmost effort to comply with said COA provision.
With reference to the subject, please be respectfully informed that the Office of the Municipal Engineer is closely coordinating with the Accounting Office and the General Services Office to determine what proper actions must be taken to comply with the recommendations mentioned.
On the other hand, this office has already submitted to the DILG the Road Network Map on October 11, 2019 and re-submitted the same on February 19,2020 after some changes/additions. This Road Network Map contains the information which are very substantial in addressing the concerns mentioned in the subject.
Hoping that with the cooperation of everyone concerned, I am confident that we will be able to comply.”

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the immediate crafting of guidelines for the scholarship program being implemented by his Office in order to ensure that the recipients are really deserving of the financial assistance being extended to elementary, high school, and college students. Said guidelines should also include the procedures on how the financial assistance may be given to students, either thru direct payment to students or a deduction of the amount payable by their respective municipal scholar to their respective school so as to ensure that said funds have served its purpose. Require from the head of all recipient public schools to submit a satisfactory explanations on why the receipt issued by them in receiving the funds due the municipal scholars were not the prescribed official receipt printed by the National Printing Office. Otherwise, require from the said school issuance of the prescribed Official Receipts for government transactions.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comments:
The Management quoted the following:
“DepEd San Juan sincerely desire to attain its millennium goal that “No one should be left behind”. In lieu of the foresaid vision but with the limited resources and funds, we must be one to create and pursue each other advocacy.
It is worthy to mention that all School Heads and allies (PTA) and other stakeholders convene annually and set some prioritization of ones’ school improvement areas. In this assembly per se, collaboration among members has been transparent and comes out that we need to construct classrooms/school buildings in Pulangbato National HS and Libato National HS amounting to ₱6,045,654.47. The underlying reasons that prompted the body were the distance and economic status of our school learner and that we could serve them better. Further, with the outgrowing number of ALS student in the area, they needed support to augment their learning activities.
In connection to the AOM and cognizant to Joint Circular No.1 s.2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG and EO No. 356, categorically we deemed to be corrected in the future implementation of the Special Education Fund. Relative to these, we will adhere to your recommendations as indicated in your AOM No.2020-009 dated July 7, 2020.”

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: 1. evaluate and secure relevant documents issued pertaining to all Financial and Trust Liabilities as well as Inter-Agency Payables, such as Accounts Payable, Due to Officers and Employees, Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable, Due to LGUs and Due to NGAs which are the long outstanding and unsubstantiated for proper disposition and de-recognition, if warranted; and 2. make proper representation in writing with the concerned agencies to confirm the existence of such obligation in their Office. If said agencies have valid claims that match with the recorded obligation of the Municipality, proceed to the processing of payment upon presentation of sufficient documentation. Otherwise, revert these accounts to Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund after securing authorization of the Sangguniang Bayan, in order to rid from the books any unsubstantiated claims, and to reflect a valid and fairly stated balances of liability accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comments:
1. Due from LGU - Province
The long outstanding balances of these were forwarded balances in the books since 1978, 1980, 1997, 2009 and 2011. Entries and/or adjustments effected in the books of the Agency (LGU-San Juan, Batangas) were based solely on Subsidiary Ledgers being requested from time to time by this Office from the Provincial Government of Batangas. This Office has no available supporting documents and/or data that would substantiate or establish the validity of said Receivables, thus in effect, hinders or precludes the Municipality from enforcement of collection.
2. Due to LGU – Province of ₱100,000.00 pertains to the Kilusang Kabuhayan at Kaunlaran (KKK) loan which was sourced out/funded by the Provincial Government. Said amount was released by the LGU to private individuals in the form of loans primarily for livelihood purposes. Kilusang Kabuhayan at Kaunlaran was established as a priority program of the government under Executive Order No. 715 dated August 06, 1981 of the then incumbent President Ferdinand E. Marcos. The primary objective of this program is for the community to achieve local development thru self-reliance and effective use of resources for livelihood activities. Functions and implementation of this program were cascaded from the national level down to the local level. In this case from Provincial level to Municipal level, with the Municipality being the primary unit responsible for the implementation of the KKK program at the local level. Accordingly, Accounts Receivable were set-up in the Trust Fund books of the Municipality for the loan released to individual debtor. Incidentally, this amount has been the subject of a request for authority to write-off from the books way back in 2004 due to absence of available data/document to enforce collection of these receivable. Likewise, efforts to collect such as sending demand letters to alleged debtor proved futile.
3. Proceeds from Sale of Certified Palay Seeds of the Municipal Agriculture Office in the amount of ₱231,480.00 is for transfer to General Fund and shall be for reversion to the Unappropriated Surplus pending issuance of authority by the Sanggunian Bayan for the purpose.
4. Inclusion of various items amounting to ₱7,232,917.63 in the Due to LGUs account
With reference to Item Nos. 1 and 2 of General Fund and Items Nos. 4, 5, 6, 7 and 10 of Trust Fund books, these amounts could have come unto the possession of the LGU for particular or specific purposes as these funds were received on the basis of ordinances, administrative orders, resolutions and Memorandum of Understanding and therefore were treated as Due to LGUs – Municipal. Accordingly, the LGU is bound to spend these funds for purposes connected with the projects. Nevertheless, adjusting/reclassification entries have been drawn to provide appropriate classification of these items in the books.
Relative to Item Nos. 7 and 10 (Trust Fund) which pertain to proceeds from payment of charges for the utilization of irrigation services (electric pump and submersible pump) amounting to ₱48,723.00 and ₱272,745.50, respectively, the implementing MOU between the LGU and the Irrigator’s Association has to undergo renewal due to change of LGU leadership in 2019. Just the same, these items together with Item Nos. 8, 9 and 11 are for transfer to the General Fund and shall be reverted to the Unappropriated Surplus pending issuance of authority for the purpose by the Local Sanggunian Bayan.
Item No. 3 (Due to LGU – Province ₱100,000.00) requires no adjustment/reclassification in the books as said amount was actually sourced out from the Provincial Government and therefore payable to said LGU.
