observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to record back to Property, Plant and Equipment all public infrastructures that
were transferred to the Registry of Public Infrastructure for fair presentation in
the financial statements and to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer and
General Services Officer in the conduct of an inventory of all public
infrastructures of the Municipality and classify the same in accordance with the
components provided in COA Circular No. 2016-004.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to direct:
a. the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record
the identified reconciling items as it appear during reconciliation process,
duly supported by validated documents from the bank, in order to adjust
the Cash in Bank and other affected accounts and reflect the correct
account balances in the year-end financial statements.
b. the Municipal Treasurer to make representation with depository banks to
secure copies of debit and credit memos as basis in recording said
reconciling items, and, henceforth, request depository banks to provide
management, regularly, of these documents as attachment to bank
statements in the future;

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to:
d) locate all the source documents and MOA with the source agencies to
review the terms and conditions corresponding to the funds transferred in
relation to the implementation of programs and projects;
e) validate if the specific programs or projects for which the funds were
transferred were already implemented, goods/services were already
delivered, liquidation of funds were already submitted and received by the
source agencies, and liabilities were already settled for proper
adjustments in the books;
f) effect the final liquidation of the funds received;
g) return to the source agency any unexpended balance; and
h) request authority for the write off of dormant fund transfer accounts in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, if
warranted, duly supported by a quarterly aging of dormant fund transfers
and other relevant documents

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the BAC to expedite the
bidding process and shall be on a “jury duty” until the Notice of Award is
issued by the HOPE in order to complete the entire procurement process at
the earliest possible time.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor that funds maintained in the Special
Trust Fund be included in the formulation of PPAs in the MDRRM Investment
Plan in accordance with regulations.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to fully institutionalize the LDRRMO
by creating additional 1 plantilla position to complete the required positions,
and, filling up the statutory/mandatory positions required under NDRRMC-
DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) 2014-1 to assure
implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and
management.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a) require the BAC to apply appropriate timing and phasing in the
procurement of LDRRM projects;
b) appoint a MDRRM officer to focus on the PPA implementation of the
DRRM of the Municipality and to implement planned
programs/projects/activities in order to improve the capacity of the
Municipality to respond to emergencies or disasters;
c) create a Monitoring Team, which may be headed by the Municipal Budget
Officer, who has the official list PPAs budgeted during the year.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor and the local sanggunian to:
a) craft ordinances and issue executive orders related to waste
management and environmental protection;
b) continuously improve current waste management system of the
Municipality, including, garbage classification, collection, and recycling;
c) add more waste recycling facilities; and
d) focus on the recycling methods of the recyclables.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer
to intensify collection and send demand letters citing the municipal ordinance
that the non-payment of fees due as provided in the ordinance is ground for
the disconnection of water connection.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Human Resource
Management Officer to adhere to the guidelines of CSC Memorandum
Circular No. 17 s. 2002 and Section 77 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to direct the GAD Focal Person to
conduct gender analysis in the formulation of programs and projects and
activities so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services,
protection and other assistance that may be provided.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer
and Municipal Accountant to exert effort to enforce immediate settlement of
the audit disallowances and charges. Likewise, ensure that all employees
who are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the
disallowances and charges for which they are liable in accordance with
Section 7 of COA Circular 2009-006 that the settlement of disallowances and
charges is made within the prescribed period.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to cause the licensing of its
ambulance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct Municipal Accountant
device control mechanism in the processing of claims to facilitate audit
action.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer
and the Municipal Accountant to adapt a monitoring mechanism to detect
variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld. Furthermore, we also
recommend that the Municipal Treasurer submit all financial transaction
documents such as the disbursement vouchers, payrolls and official receipts
to the accounting section on or before the 5th day of the month so that
accounting records such as check disbursement journal, cash disbursement
journal, journal of collections and deposits, general journals, general and
subsidiary ledgers could be updated monthly to afford the accounting section
to review and reconcile the exact amount to be remitted to the Bureau of
Internal Revenue.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require:
a) the Municipal Treasurer to remit the GSIS contributions and loans on or
before the dates prescribed by GSIS;
b) the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to adapt a monitoring
mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and
withheld;
c) the Municipal Accountant to regularly update accounting records such as
cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement
journal, general and subsidiary ledgers in order to determine the exact
amount to be remitted to the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a) the Municipal Treasurer to fully remit the Philhealth contributions withheld;
b) the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to adapt a monitoring
mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld;
and
c) the Municipal Accountant to regularly update accounting records such as
cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement
journal, general and subsidiary ledgers in order to determine the exact
amount to be remitted to the PHIC.

We recommended that:
a) the Municipal Treasurer remit in full the Pag-IBIG contributions on o
before the dates prescribed by HDMF;
b) the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant adapt a monitoring
mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld;
and,
c) the Municipal Accountant regularly update accounting records such as
cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement
journal, general and subsidiary ledgers in order to determine the exact
amount to be remitted to the HDMF.

observations.managementAction: Recommendations were noted and prospectively complied with. Management to ensure DVs are duly and properly supported before payments are made. Guidelines to process financial assistance to be set. Some items remain on-going.

observations.recommendation:
Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) 

The balance of the PPE account amounting to P3.276 billion is unreliable due to (a) unreconciled account balances between the accounting records and the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); (b) inclusion and exclusion of items of PPE account in either the CAD records or in the RPCPPE; (c) beginning balances without supporting details; and (d) misclassification of PPE items in the books of accounts totaling P10.977 million, resulting  in a significant positive and negative variances of P804.107 million and P454.484 million, respectively, or a net variance of P349.623 million. Moreover, unserviceable properties totaling P27.680 million were not disposed during the year. | Reiterated in Finding No. 1 |  | 

We recommended that:

The CAD:

Account the noted variances between the books of accounts and RPCPPE;
observations.reason: The noted variances in CY 2017 still subsist in the audit of CY 2018 accounts since no reconciliation was made.

observations.recommendation: Investigate the recorded, as well as unrecorded, PPE items in the books of account. Obtain sufficient proof for their existence or non-existence, and effect adjustments in the books of accounts, if necessary;
observations.reason: -do-

observations.recommendation: Exert effort to provide details on the PPE items recorded and lumped as beginning balances. If no longer possible, the procedures in the dropping off of these items from the books may be recoursed;
observations.reason: PPE items recorded and lumped as beginning balances are still in the books of accounts. No request of dropping off has been undertaken by the CAD.

observations.recommendation: Reconcile the recorded PPE with the RPCPPE. If there are discrepancies between the records, conduct strict scrutiny especially those material in amounts;
observations.reason: Reconciliation of records has not been undertaken by the CAD and GSD.

observations.recommendation: Reconcile the RPCPPE with the CAD records to update the lacking information on the date of acquisition and to properly classify the PPE items.
observations.reason: The noted variances in CY 2017 still subsist in the audit of CY 2018 accounts since no reconciliation was made.

observations.recommendation: Strictly apply the Perpetual Method in recording inventory purchases and outflow of the same; and
observations.reason: Management still applies the combination of perpetual method and outright expense method.

observations.recommendation:
Revenue

The recorded cash revenue  in the Government Appropriations and Accounting Monitoring System (GAAMS) amounting to P951.363 million did not reconcile with the revenue collections totaling P958.434 million due to different account codes and names used in the Treasury Operations and Income Monitoring System (TOIMS), thereby, showing a variance of P7.071 million. Moreover, the correctness of the recorded Business Tax for CY 2017 with a total amount of  P705.183 million could not be ascertained due to the (a) different method of applying the schedule of tax rates as prescribed in the City Revenue Code of 2013; and (b) continued absence of a detailed SLs for each business tax category. |  |  | 

We recommended that:

The CTD: 

Reconcile with the CAD the variances noted in the various Revenue accounts per CTD reports amounting to P7,070,657;
observations.reason: The issues will be corrected in CY 2019.  The changes, if made in the middle of the year might cause juggling of transactions per account.

observations.recommendation: Classify tax revenues, fees, service and  business income collected by the CTD by type/category in accordance with the prescribed category of income in the Revised Chart of Accounts (RCA) for LGUs and accordingly incorporate such classification in the TOIMS;
observations.reason: -do-

observations.recommendation: Counter check the computation/assessment of the annual tax levied on the various business establishments based on the supporting documents provided by the BPLO before collecting the tax; and
observations.reason: -do-

observations.recommendation: The BPLO apply the tax rates on all applications for business permit in accordance with the provisions of the CSJRC Code;
observations.reason: -do-

observations.recommendation:
The CAD:

Prepare and maintain detailed SLs for all accounts in accordance with the RCA for LGUs to facilitate proper recording and reporting of financial transactions;
observations.reason: -do-

observations.recommendation: Use the proper classification in the recording of collections based on the source documents and as reported by the CTD to avoid misclassification of accounts.
observations.reason: -do-

observations.recommendation: The Human Resource Department comply with the provision of the contract by maintaining nine personnel in the Administrative Office of the Arena to monitor compliance of the Operator to the provisions of the contract, and to the laws and ordinances of the City, to protect the interest of the City;
observations.reason: Management did not assign additional personnel in the Administrative Office of the Arena.

observations.recommendation: Demand from the LSB the refund of the amounts pertaining to expenses charged to SEF, otherwise, the transactions will be suspended / disallowed in audit with the City officials as the persons responsible / liable for approving the said transactions;
observations.reason: The recommendation was not acted upon since Management still insists that these expenses should be charged to SEF.

observations.recommendation: The City Budget Officer (CBO) to restrict the allocation and prioritization of the SEF to the programs and projects defined under the law, rules and regulations in order to maximize the benefits intended for the public elementary and secondary schools which could be derived from this fund.
observations.reason: The same issue still exists. The CBO did not restrict the allocation of the SEF during CY 2018.

observations.recommendation:
TF-Bottom-up Budgeting

The delayed and/or non-implementation of projects amounting to P4.00 million funded by the National Government Agencies under the BuB program defeats the City’s objective to rehabilitate its educational and health facilities through poverty reduction programs and projects as embodied in the City’s CY 2016 Local Poverty Reduction Action Plan (LPRAP), thus, depriving the constituents of the benefits that could have been derived from the said projects. | Reiterated in Finding No. 17 |  | 

We recommended that Management: 

Strictly monitor the timely implementation of the priority projects as targeted and planned in the LPRAP, being the implementing agency of the funds granted by the National Government;
observations.reason: Implementation of the project is still delayed.

observations.recommendation: Ensure that all the funds granted are fully utilized and projects are fully implemented so that the immediate desired benefits from such projects could be enjoyed by the City’s beneficiaries/constituents;
observations.reason: Not all TF were fully utilized.

observations.recommendation: Submit the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) for the BuB projects to the City Auditor to support the documentary requirements for the transfer of funds from the source agency. Henceforth, see to it that every grant from other
observations.reason: Management did not submit MOA as of December 31, 2018.

observations.recommendation: government agencies is supported by a MOA; and
Implement immediately the PPAs so as not to defeat the purpose of establishing the BUB programs which is to decentralize and facilitate the execution of the projects from NGAs to the LGUs.
observations.reason: Implementation of the project is still delayed.

observations.recommendation:
The City Engineer to:

Properly monitor the implementation of all awarded projects to avoid delayed implementation of the same; and
observations.reason: The recommendation was not acted upon by Management.

observations.recommendation:
Gender and Development (GAD) Fund

The City’s GAD Budget for CY 2017 amounting to P93.67 million is 5.43 percent of its total budget appropriations authorized under the Annual Budget and corresponds to the activities that support GAD programs, projects, hence, compliant with the costing and allocation of GAD Budget provided for under the Magna Carta of Women (MCW). However, proper planning for the PPAs to be charged thereto was not undertaken since (a) the City’s Gender Development Code could not as yet be considered as an effective tool in the CY 2017 GAD Plan and Budget (GPB); (b) sex-disaggregation has yet to be completed; and (c) out of 1,070 City employees, only 218 or 20 percent had undergone gender capacity building, thus, the efficiency and effectiveness in the identification of the relevant PPAs for the City’s GAD GPB could not be optimally achieved. | Reiterated in Finding No. 20 |  | 

We recommended that:

The Sangguniang Panlungsod:

Review the GAD Code as to its relevance to current gender issues and concerns in the locality based on updated consultation with women constituents and the women’s empowerment and gender equality agenda of the government in accordance with the Magna Carta of Women and recommend amendments to improve/update its provisions; and
observations.reason: The recommendation was not given due attention in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: Promulgate the IRR on the City GAD Code of 2017 in accordance with Section 28 of City Ordinance No. 82 series of 2017, to ensure the effective implementation of the Ordinance.
observations.reason: The recommendation was not given due attention in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: Indicate all the required information/data in the signboards to inform the public of the essential features/elements of the PPAs; and
observations.reason: Same issue still exists.

observations.recommendation: Inform COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the implementation of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted.
observations.reason: Management did not inform COA of information on the posting of signboards and/or public notices within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the implementation of the PPAs.

observations.recommendation: The City Engineer to monitor implementation of the projects on the agreed time frame in the contracts to avoid delay in the delivery of services to the public; and
observations.reason: The implementation of the projects during CY 2018 was not monitored by the City Engineer, hence, there were PPAs which were delayed or not implemented.

Compliance with Tax Laws and Other Regulatory Requirements

Due to BIR, Pag-IBIG, Philhealth and GSIS

Account for all the variances noted in audit relative to the accounts Due to BIR, GSIS, Philhealth and Pag-IBIG;

observations.recommendation:
1.5 In view of the noted observations, we have recommended that
management implement the following measures to ensure the reliability
of the balances of PPE accounts and records:

1.5.1 Prepare the physical inventory Reports in accordance with the
prescribed form and in complete/correct information;

1.5.2 Submit the final inventory reports to the Property Officer for
verification and reconciliation with the records maintained by the
Accounting Office and property cards of the property unit;

1.5.3 Establishment of property identification and coding to ease
inventory taking;

1.5.4 Maintenance of the updated and complete Acknowledgement
Receipt of Equipment to establish the official accountable for the
property;

1.5.5 Reclassification of unserviceable property noted during the
physical count of inventory to Other Assets and conduct a proper
disposal of unserviceable property; and

1.5.6 PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards should be maintained and
updated by the respective offices in accordance with the NGAS
Manual for LGU.

2.7 We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:
 the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and
Road Map and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card
for all roads and its components;
 the Municipal Accountant to trace the recorded road, highways and
bridges which were closed to government equity prior to 2016 and
subsequently record in its books of accounts the amount of road
works equivalent to the required transition provision, keep and
maintain Registry of Public Infrastructures for Road and subsidiary
records for roads and road components for every road network and
provide for the depreciation for the local road network; and
 the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the
General Services Officer with the complete description and cost
segregation of road components for road projects.

3.3 We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant take the proposed
adjusting entries to better present the financial statements of the
Municipality.

4.8 In view of the above observations, we have recommended the following:
 the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to trace back
the source of the discrepancies of the Cash in Bank account balances
and record all book reconciling items to bring the balances into
agreement so that the correct balance of cash is presented in the
financial statements;
 the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer verify from the
bank the details of all debit and credit memos reflected in the bank
statements to record correctly these transactions; and
 verified reconciling items should be taken up immediately after the
preparation of the monthly reconciliation statements.

5.7 We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
5.7.1 exert effort to analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other
Funds accounts, trace back the transactions in prior years and
determine the differences and the nature of the accounts.
5.7.2 prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct
balances of the accounts.
5.7.3 maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary.

6.7 We have recommended that the following measures be undertaken by
management:
 The Municipal Treasurer exert extra effort to enforce the collection of
the delinquent RPT/SET receivables. Consider imposing the remedies
provided under Section 247 of the Local Government Code.
 The Municipal Assessor furnishes the Accountant copy of certified
list of taxpayers at the beginning of the year as basis for recording
RPT and SET Receivable.
 The Municipal Accountant and Treasurer maintain data as to the
composition of the uncollected RPT and SET receivables. Issue
demand letter if necessary.

7.5 In view of the above observations, we have recommended that the:
 Municipal Treasurer remit immediately the amount due to the BIR
within the required period; and
 Management submit the financial records to support the claim for
adjustment from 2013 and prior liabilities to BIR.

8.6 We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer coordinate and
closely monitor the check preparation and remittance of GSIS
withholdings, to facilitate prompt preparation of check payment and the
remittance thereof, and also to avoid ineligibility of members from
availing of the benefits and privileges provided by the said agency.
Management should be reminded of the penalties and/or effect of delayed
remittance of mandatory obligations.

8.7 We have also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer see to it that
the amounts deducted from each employee should also be the amount
remitted to GSIS and also, they should also check the official receipt
because some amounts reflected in the official receipt from GSIS do not
tally with amounts in the remittance list.

9.5 We have recommended that all perfected contracts and purchase orders
be submitted to COA within five days after perfection thereof for the
immediate review of the reasonableness of the cost, conduct of inspection
and other necessary audit procedures.

10.5 We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
10.5.1 Submit justification on the numerous cancelled checks Refrain/
avoid cancelling checks when they are not actually torn, mutilated,
defaced or with erasures/errors pursuant to rules and regulations
of the NGAS;
10.5.2 Submit the eleven cancelled checks for audit. Henceforth, exercise
due care in the succeeding issuances of checks to avoid
unnecessary wastage of government funds.

11.4 We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor effect immediately the
closure of the bank accounts at Philippine National Bank  Bangued
Branch and transfer the existing account balance to any Authorized
Government Depository Banks (AGDBs) as enumerated by DOF Circular
No. 01-2017 dated May 11, 2017.

13.9 Install adequate internal control system in the handling of cash and
collections. Require the Municipal Treasurer to remit their collections at the
close of each business day or not later than the following day unless the travel
time from their places of assignment is more than one day, which in such
case, the turnover of collections shall be made at least once a week or as soon
as the collections reach ₱5,000.00. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain
updated cashbook and post the collections and remittances/deposits as they
occur.

13.10 We have recommended the Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections
regularly and to deposit the balance of ₱6,475.67 as of December 31, 2018
pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on NGAS, Section 111 of GAAM
Vol. I and DBM-DOF Joint Cir. 1-81.

14.6 We have recommended that in the preparation of the project proposal to
be submitted to the DBM, the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator should see to it that the programs, projects and activities
included in the plan should be specific for easier implementation and
monitoring.

15.11 We have recommended that the:
15.11.1 Municipal Accountant submit the noted lacking documents for
the audit team to evaluate and establish the validity of the
disbursements;
15.11.2 Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant ensure that all
documentary requirements supporting all procurement and
disbursements are present; and;
15.11.3 Processing and payment of claims that are not properly
documented should be discontinued.

16.5 We have recommended the management to submit to office of the auditor
proofs of the actual receipt duly signed and acknowledged by the
individual beneficiaries showing the items and quantity actually received.

17.6 We have recommended the Municipality to follow up the Office of the
Presidential Adviser on the Peace Process the amount of ₱ 316,000.00 for
the remaining payment of the delivered farm inputs and implements.

18.4 We have recommended that members of the BAC, TWG and BAC
secretariat ensure the use and completion of the standard forms of the
PBDs.

19.4 We have recommended that the BAC invite observers in writing in all the
stages of the procurement process in accordance with the provisions of
the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184. Likewise, the BAC should also
see to it that the observers invited do not have direct or indirect interest.
Ensure that the documents attached are properly received with correct
dates.

20.4 We have recommended that the Municipality adhere to the governing
rules on the sale of bidding documents to all interested bidders as
provided in the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

21.5.8 We have recommended that the members of the BAC submit
justification/explanation on the noted violations and ensure that
prospective bidders submit complete eligibility requirements and
post the necessary bid security before they are marked as passed and
post-audited.

22.3 We have recommended that the members of the BAC submit
justification/explanation on the noted violations and ensure that winning
bidders submit complete documentary requirements prior to entering
into contract.

23.5 We have recommended that the Municipal Government, through the
BAC Secretariat comply with the posting requirements on all its
procurement. Notices of Award and Proceed, and the approved contract
be posted in the PhilGEPS website and website of the municipality, if any,
within the reglementary period as required by the Revised IRR of R.A.
No. 9184.

24.3 We have recommended that the BAC strictly comply with the procedures
on the receipt, opening and preliminary examination of bids as provided
for in the Procurement Manual for LGUs and the 2016 Revised IRR of
R.A. No. 9184.

25.6 We have recommended that management:
a. Instruct the Project Facilitator to submit a justification for certifying the
project as 100 percent accomplished and in accordance with plans and
specifications when work item PCCP was not executed properly;
b. require the concerned contractor to employ corrective measures on the
concrete works. Otherwise, demand the refund of the cost of the
deficiencies;
c. conduct inspection of the project upon notice of the contractor that said
deficiency was rectified to determine whether corrective measures were
satisfactorily made;
d. submit request for re-inspection of the rectified project to the Auditor
for appropriate action.

26.4 In view of the above observation, we have recommended that:
Management determine the cost of the unperformed portion of the
works for every day delay and compute the amount of liquidated
damages in accordance with the pertinent provisions of R.A. 9184 and
the contract agreement; and
 That the Municipal Mayor enforce immediate collection of liquidated
damages and ensure the refund of the excess of the bill of quantities
form the contract price from the contractor.

27.5 We have recommended that the Municipal officials concerned maximize
the utilization of the 20% Development Fund to attain the desirable socio-
economic developments and environmental outcomes for the municipality
and its intended beneficiaries.

28.3.1.3 We have recommended that management submit monthly Report
on the Receipt and Utilization of the DRRMF sourced from GAA
and Report on the Receipt of the municipality to NDRRMC
through the OCD on or before the 5th day following the end of
each month, and furnish a copy to the audit team for
audit/evaluation.

28.3.2.7 We have recommended that Municipal officials implement their
LDRRMP as mandated by law to lessen the socio-economic and
environmental impacts of disaster in the community and disaster
risks, including projected climate risks, and to enhance disaster
preparedness and response capabilities of the community.

28.3.3.8 We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
28.3.3.8.1 take up under the Welfare Goods for Distribution
Inventory account the items for distribution to end-
users/beneficiaries properly supported with RIS and
listings of recipients/beneficiaries duly signed
acknowledging receipt showing the items and quantity of
items actually received.
28.3.3.8.2 exercise due care in posting the appropriate account in
the preparation of the working trial balances to avoid
posting to a different account.

29.5 We have recommended that the Municipal Government identify the GAD
PPAs through the use of HGDG which would be the basis in the budget
determination depending on its gender responsiveness or attribute to
GAD interventions.

30.3 We have recommended that management instruct the GAD Focal Person
to see to it that the funding requirement for GAD is complied with.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The General Services Department to dispose the accounted unserviceable properties in accordance with the procedures prescribed by the existing rules and regulations, either through public auction, sale through negotiation, donation or destruction, as the case warrants, to generate additional income therefrom which could be used for other more productive projects; and
The City Accounting Department to effect the necessary adjusting entries to drop the subject unserviceable properties from the books, after the disposal of the same.

We recommended that Management instruct the General Services Department to:
Prepare and submit to the GSIS an updated Property Inventory Form to arrive at the correct assessment of all the risks attached to the insurable properties of the City by the GSIS Insurance Group using the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 92-390 dated November 17, 1992;
Insure all PPE of the City, except those unserviceable, as prescribed by RA 656, COA Circular Nos. 92-390 and 2018-002; and
Monitor and facilitate timely renewal of the insurance policies in order to avoid lapsing of the same.

We recommended that the City Treasurer and the City Accountant facilitate a regular reconciliation of the Cashbook and the GL balances to ensure the completeness and accuracy of the recorded transactions and balances of the cash accounts, and to make adjusting entries, where appropriate.

We recommended that Management instruct the CTD to:
Determine the monthly estimated cash requirements of the City to establish the estimated cash balance that would be recommended for investments to enable proper utilization of cash resources and maximize the income that could be derived from the said investments; and
Inquire from time to time with other authorized government depository banks that offer higher interests earnings on time deposit investments.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the City Treasurer to monitor/update the fidelity bonds and amounts of accountabilities of its accountable officers to conform with the requirements set forth under Section 5.1 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The CTD to establish first the correct amount in the Status of Unredeemed Real Property Units Sold thru Public Auction before submission to the CAD for recording in the books under the Trust Liabilities account; and
The CAD to regularly reconcile the balance of Real Property Auction under the Trust Liabilities account with the records of the CTD.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The CAD to transfer the recorded SHT in the Trust Fund to a Special Account in the General Fund;
The CTD to submit to the DILG, HUDCC and DOF annual report stating the projected generated income and prospective projects to be funded by the SHT; and
The Sangguniang Panlungsod to facilitate amendment of the Revenue Code to include the commencement and termination of the implementation and imposition of the SHT.

We recommended that Management:
Publish in the official website of the City the tax declaration numbers of all delinquent taxpayers to readily apprise the concerned taxpayers of their obligation; and
Instruct the CTD to exert more effort in the collection of delinquent taxes, especially those due for over 10 years, otherwise, apply the provision of Section 2A.52 of the CSJRC regarding penalty for failure to issue and execute warrant of levy.

We recommended that the CTD seek assistance from the Securities and Exchange Commission and/or the Bureau of Internal Revenue in gathering financial information to determine/verify the amounts of gross sales/receipts being declared by business owners to facilitate the conduct of examination of the books of accounts of the taxpayers, whether with big or small businesses.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly enforce collection of outstanding receivables from the City’s officers and employees who are still in the service. For those employees who will resign or retire from the service, effect the deductions from their claims for terminal leave credits.  For those who have already resigned or were retired with unpaid loan balances, issue demand letters for the settlement thereof. Otherwise, require the refund of the amounts from the officials and employees responsible for the grant of clearances;
For cash shortages, enforce the immediate settlement of the same; and
Exert extra effort to provide details or breakdown as to the nature of the account Due from Officers and Employees recorded in the books under the SEF and TF.

We recommended that Management:
Cause the refund of the amount of P6.349 million from the GF to SEF to enable the City to use the fund for its intended purpose;
Stop charging expenditures against the SEF which are not allowed under the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01; and
Ensure that the LSB will be guided by the provisions of RA 7160 and the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular in the preparation of the SEF Annual Budget for identification of priority expenditures chargeable therefrom.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management utilize the 20% DF within the scheduled period of implementation as indicated in the Annual Investment Program so as not to deprive the constituents and the City of the benefits which could be derived thru the timely implementation of the funded projects, and to improve the socio-economic condition of the City.

We recommended that Management:
Stop charging against the LDRRMF those projects and activities that are not included or related to the DRRM activities as stated under Section 21 of RA 10121 and Section 4.0 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
Refund from the General Fund to the Trust Liability-DRRM account the amount of P15.780 million representing the inappropriate utilization of the LDRRMF.
We recommended that Management include the list of PPAs charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the LDRRMFIP in compliance with Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-02.

We recommended that Management:
Prioritize the identification of a lot for the City’s Central Materials Recovery and Composting Facility, and lots within every Barangay where MRFs could be established for the sorting, segregation and composting of waste materials; and
Advise each Barangay to encourage its members to comply with the requirements of RA 9003 in terms of reducing, recycling and reusing garbage.

We recommended that Management instruct the CGFPS to exert more effort in the proper monitoring of the implementation of the planned PPAs to ensure the judicious utilization of the GAD budget.
We recommended that Management instruct the CGFPS to set up and maintain appropriate systems and mechanisms that would ensure the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-disaggregated data or GAD database considering that this will serve as basis in the performance-based and gender-responsive planning and budgeting.
We recommended that Management:
Review the GAD Code as to its relevance to current gender issues and concerns in the locality based on updated consultation with women constituents in accordance with the Magna Carta of Women and recommend amendments to improve/update its provisions; and
Promulgate the IRR on the City GAD Code of 2017 in accordance with Section 28 of City Ordinance No. 82 series of 2017 to ensure the effective implementation of the said Ordinance.
We recommended that Management instruct the City Human Resources Management Officer (CHRMO) to require all personnel to undergo training on gender sensitivity, human rights and gender responsiveness especially those holding positions involving protection and defense of women against gender-based violence, counselling and trauma healing.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Engineering Department to:
Display/post the prescribed signboards containing the required information/data in the place where the PPAs are located to adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 on the publicity and other forms of announcement to the public pertaining to the PPAs undertaken by the City; and
Inform COA within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted and submit, at the beginning of every year, the required list of all on-going PPAs and those that are to be implemented for the year pursuant to Items 2.1, 3.1 and 3.2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.
We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all planned infrastructure projects are implemented within the period scheduled in the Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accounting Department to:
Submit immediately the lacking DVs complete with relevant supporting documents, otherwise, cause the suspension of payment of salaries of the employees responsible for the delayed and non-submission of the required records until the same shall have been complied pursuant to Section 122(2) of PD 1445;
Prepare/render a separate report on unreleased/unclaimed checks and include cancelled checks in the RCI to be submitted to the Audit Team on a monthly basis together with the Summary of Disbursements; and
Ensure that disbursements are supported with complete documents before effecting payment to avoid audit disallowance in the future.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to ensure remittance of actual taxes and contributions withheld from the salaries of the City’s personnel on the month following the month in which withholding was made to avoid any variances.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to recheck/review the accuracy of the total amount of withheld taxes/other contributions and the amount to be remitted to the concerned agencies every month to avoid over/under remmittance.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to recheck/review the accuracy of the total amount of withheld taxes/other contributions and the amount to be remitted to the concerned agencies every month to avoid over/under remmittance.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to recheck/review the accuracy of the total amount of withheld taxes/other contributions and the amount to be remitted to the concerned agencies every month to avoid over/under remmittance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal
Treasurer reconcile their records and effect adjustments so as to ascertain
the accuracy and correctness of the account Cash-Local Treasury and for its
fair presentation in the financial statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare monthly
Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) in accordance with COA Circular No.
96-011 and give the Auditor a copy. Meanwhile, we urge him to itemize or
determine, if possible the unreconciled figures to establish the correct
36
accountabilities or balances of the accounts being maintained by the
municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Assessor and officials concerned to obtain Transfer Certificate of Title for all
parcels of land acquired by the municipality to serve as proof of absolute
ownership and to be effective with third parties.

We recommended that the OIC Municipal Accountant, Municipal
Engineer, General Services Officer and Inventory Committee enjoin and
complete the recognition of Public Infrastructures to Road Network account
so as to comply with the prescribed transitory provisions in COA Circular
No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the
accounts Due To Other Funds and Due From Other Funds and prepare
adjusting/correcting entries, if necessary. Likewise, transfer of funds from
one fund to another fund should accordingly be recorded in the books of
accounts of both funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert those that
are long outstanding payables in accordance with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
Subsidiary ledgers and all available records to support the amount to be
reverted should be attached to the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) that has to
43
be prepared for the reversion to the unappropriated surplus. In the event
that there may be claims in the future, the JEV should serve as the basis in
asking for a supplemental budget for the settlement of the payable.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Accountant return immediately the unutilized Priority Development
Assistance Fund (PDAF) to the National Treasury.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer require the submission of Daily Time Record certified correct by
the employee concerned and approved by his immediate supervisor before
paying their salaries and wages. We also recommended that the Municipal
Accountant submit the DTRs to the Auditor for verification and for proper
monitoring of attendance of every employee. Likewise, in the same manner,
submit attendance sheets of the Presiding Officer and Sangguniang Bayan
members in Sanggunian Sessions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer return the unutilized funds to the Bureau of Treasury or to the
source agencies to fully liquidate the accounts in their books and so that
they can re-appropriate the funds for other projects.

We recommended that the MDRRMO submit Monthly Report on the
Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund to the COA
Auditor in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12,
2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that all
required documents for each kind of transaction are attached to DVs and
properly filled up thus ensuring the validity and propriety of all transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer promptly remit RPT and SET share of the Provincial Government
of Ilocos Sur in accordance with the above provisions.

We recommended that the BAC require the submission from the
winning bidders/contractors the duly approved Construction Safety and
Health Program which will become part of the perfected contracts upon its
submission for the contract review. Pending the approval by the DOLE of
the program on the date of submission of perfected contracts for review, a
certification that the program has been submitted for approval by the DOLE
should be attached. Upon the final payment of these contracts, the Municipal
Accountant should require said duly approved program. This is to ensure
compliance with R.A. 9184 and issuance of the DOLE.

We recommended that management promulgate and implement
programs, projects and activities that address gender issues and women’s
concerns and to fully implement them.

We recommended that the municipal government intensify the
collection of real property taxes. Likewise, the Municipal treasurer should
issue notice of delinquency to all delinquent taxpayers for them to settle all
their taxes due so as to augment income of the municipality that could be
used to finance priority programs and projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the immediate
implementation of the funded projects so that the benefits derived from these
projects will be timely delivered to the constituents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the immediate
implementation of funded projects so that benefits derived from them will be
timely delivered to the constituents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure the optimal
utilization of the 20% Development Fund to help achieve desirable socio-
economic and environmental outcomes for the municipality and its intended
beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Council for the Protection of
Children see to it that the 1% of IRA allocated for the protection of children
be fully utilized and programs, projects and activities be consistent with the
activities provided in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July
4, 2012.

We recommended that the management settle/appeal disallowance
within six (6) months from receipt of the notice. Furthermore, the Municipal
Accountant should ensure that supporting documents of transactions are
complete, proper and valid in order to prevent the same from being
suspended/disallowed in post-audit.

We reiterated our recommendation and management agreed that the Inventory Committee conduct physical count of property, plant and equipment annually and submit a report thereon not later than January 31 of each year and the Accounting and Property Office to maintain a complete subsidiary records for these properties. We also recommended that the Accounting Office exert extra effort to locate the records of the unreconciled balance in order to establish the accuracy of the affected PPE accounts and for its fair presentation in the financial statements.

We have recommended and management agreed that the LCE enjoin the Inventory Committee to:
a.   Include in the conduct of inventory of the properties of the Municipality the public infrastructures and other community facilities;
b.   Prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Road Network System.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Property Officer-Designate prepare and maintain complete subsidiary ledgers and  local road network property cards, respectively for all roads and its components.

We have recommended that Management refrain from granting cash advances to Municipal officials and employees and other personnel other than for an authorized official travel.   Strict adherence to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances is also recommended.

We have recommended and Management agreed that through the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), all purchases of various supplies and materials be made through the general mode of procurement which is public bidding to do away with the practice of reimbursement. If competitive bidding cannot be ultimately pursued, the BAC through a resolution, shall justify and recommend the change on the method of procurement to be approved by the Local Chief Executive. Strict adherence to the provision under COA Circular No. 97-002 regarding the Imprest Fund System in order to have a more efficient and effective internal control is also enjoined.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
1.  BAC to strictly comply with the requirements provided in the above-mentioned regulations in the procurement of drugs and medicines;
2.  Inventory Committee to include in the conduct of inventory the drugs and medicines to establish the accountability of the Accountable Officer; and
3. Accounting Office to make the necessary adjustments for its fair presentation in the financial statements.

We have recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive, as head of the Procuring Entity, ensure that prior to contract signing for infrastructure projects, require the winning bidder to submit CSHP approved by the DOLE which forms part of contract in order to promote the welfare of the contruction workers.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
a. Municipal Engineer to notify the contractors about the defects and require them to perform corrective actions to prolong the life of LGU’s infrastructure projects;
b. The Municipal Engineer to ensure that the billboards displayed for projects will be fabricated and posted properly by the contractor  based on COA Circular No. 2013-04 for transparency and to avoid accident; and
c. Project Monitoring Committee to perform regular and thorough monitoring and supervision of projects to ensure that only those satisfactorily completed and in accordance with the specifications are paid.

We have recommended and management agreed that all Department Heads involved in the development projects monitor the implementation of proposed programs and projects to ensure that funds allocated are fully utilized for projects identified in the Annual Development Plan.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Development Council regularly convene to update or modify the priority development projects as called for under existing circumstances and also changing conditions in the locality.

We have recommended that management cause the immediate implementation of the programmed infrastructure projects under the RA 7171 fund to support the tobacco farmers of the municipality and help them improve their way of life. Further, that management judiciously plan the timeline of the procurement process and implementation of programs/projects/activities of the LGU to maximize benefits that could be derived by the farmers.

We have recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant include in the presentation of SCBAA, the appropriation and actual obligation of prior years’ or continuing appropriations for RA No. 7171 for transparency and proper presentation of the accountability of the Agency over its resources.

We have recommended and Management acceded that the HoPE direct the BAC to prepare the Project Monitoring Report and approve by him and submit to the GPPB in compliance with Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) cause or order the Municipal Treasurer to collect these long outstanding receivables particularly the accounts with available reference documents and instruct the Municipal Accountant thru the help of the Municipal Treasurer to exert extra effort to locate the documents pertaining to the long outstanding receivables. For the receivables where settlement/ collectibility could no longer be ascertained, the LCE can file the request for debt relief or authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We have recommended that Management ensure that funds allocated for Special Education Fund be budgeted and utilized strictly in accordance with R.A. No. 7160 and DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01, s. 2017 in order to attain the intended purpose for which the fund was created. We further recommend that the amount of P335,048.90 be charged against the General Fund.

We have recommended that management refrain from charging PPAs which are not directly related to disaster risk reduction and management activities to the LDRRM Fund and the Municipal Accounting and Budget Office prepare the necessary adjustments of these expenditures in the books and to strictly comply with the provisions of RA 10121, COA Circular No. 2012-002 and JMC No.2013-1.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRMC to convene in order to review and reprogram unutilized QRF and DRRMF – MOOE for the approval of the Municipal Development Council.

We have recommended that Management (a) direct the GAD Focal Point System to administer the HGDG tests for the formulation of GAD PPAs that will specifically address the gender gaps/issues and concerns; (b) for the LCE to issue an Executive Order for the establishment of monitoring and evaluation system through the creation of Monitoring and Evaluation Team that will evaluate the results of the Municipality’s GAD policies, programs and projects; and (c) management maximize the utilization of the GAD fund on  programs/projects/ activities that address gender issues and concerns.

We have recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accounting Office review, confirm and verify all information available to establish the validity and existence of these dormant payables and adjust the accounts accordingly in line with the objective of fair presentation of liabilities in the financial statements.  We further recommend that the Municipal Accounting Office maintain complete and reliable subsidiary ledgers and conduct regular monitoring of outstanding liabilities.

We have recommended that Management appropriate in the Annual Budget at least one percent (1%) of their IRA for LCPC programs, projects and activities as required under Section 15 of RA 9344. We recommend further that the design of PPAs should be in accordance with the enumeration under Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.

We have recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the RPT/SET Receivables account from non-current to current receivables for fair presentation in the Financial Statements.

We have recommended that the Municipality stop the practice of issuing checks in the name of the school principals but rather on the name of school and comply with pertinent COA Circulars.
Thereafter, ensure that grants provided to schools are duly acknowledged, recognized in the books and used based on its intended purpose.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management cause strict compliance to COA Circular 2009-1 on the timely submission of contract with all its supporting documents by the BAC Chairperson/Secretariat to the Auditor within five (5) days upon execution/issuance of the contracts and to submit immediately all the necessary supporting documents in order for review and evaluation by the audit team.

We have recommended that Management adopt measures that would facilitate the submission of their accounts on time as prescribed in the foregoing Sections of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 and P.D. 1445.
We further recommended that additional staff with accounting background be assigned at the Accounting Office in order to address the delay on the submission of accounts.

We recommended that Management immediately settle the aforesaid tax penalties and surcharges to avoid further distrain or levy upon the personal and real properties of the Municipality and accumulation of interests and surcharges to the detriment of the funds of the LGU.
We have recommended that management through the Municipal Accountant, exert best efforts to substantiate the account and cause the immediate settlement and payment of the above disallowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall direct:

 •  The Inventory Committee  to conduct the actual count of  all  properties
   of the municipality;
 • The Municipal Treasurer  to prepare the report  on  the  results  of  the
   count using the prescribed form and reconcile the  same  with  accounting
   records  so  that  the  correctness  of  the  account  balances  can   be
   ascertained.
  •  The  Municipal  Treasurer  as  Property  Custodian  and  the  Municipal
    Accountant to maintain stock cards and ledgers  cards,  respectively  to
    support the actual amount of  PPE  to  be  reflected  in  the  financial
    statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should exert effort  to  update
the  preparation  and  submission  of  the   subject   bank   reconciliation
statements. Henceforth, strictly  observed  the  mandated  timeline  on  the
submission of the same report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal  Treasurer  to
release the 25%  barangay  share  from  Real  Property  Tax  collections  in
accordance with Section 271 (d) of RA 7160, so that these  can  be  utilized
for priority projects of the barangay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Disbursing  Officer  to
gather all the approved payrolls job order  workers  for  payment  of  wages
covering the same period  and  to  prepare  one  cash  advance  voucher  and
correspondingly issue one check for such payments which is  most  economical
and not voluminous on the part of Management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the Municipal Accountant to
strictly observe the timely  preparation  and  submission  of  monthly  and
quarterly  financial  reports  with  complete  schedules   and   supporting
documents in order to provide relevant and timely information essential  in
decision making.

We recommended  that  Management  strictly  adhere  the  provisions  of  COA
Circular No. 2009-001 in the procurement of infrastructure  projects.  Since
the prescribed period of five (5) working days has already  lapsed  instruct
the  BAC  Secretariat  to  submit  immediately  to  the  Auditor  copies  of
perfected contracts and supporting  documents  of  the  above  projects  for
auditorial, legal and technical review.

We recommended that  Management  require  the  suppliers  to  post  Warranty
Security  in  the  amount  provided  under  Section  62.1  of  the   Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184  in the procurement  of  goods
in order to fully protect the interest of the procuring agency  in  case  of
manufacturing defects on the delivered goods.

We recommended that Management shall closely monitor  the  timely  and  full
utilization of the current year’s 70% Prevention  and  Mitigation  Fund  and
continuing appropriations of LDRRMF  for  the  implementation  of  projects,
programs and activities incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan in  order
to maximize the support for  disaster  risk  management  activities  of  the
Municipality.

1. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer
to reconcile the PPE account of the Municipality; and to record the Child
Development Center Building in the books;
2. Direct the GSO to include in the RPCPPE, PPE items funded by the SEF
and to provide complete details of the PPE items in the Property Cards for
record and monitoring purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
submit the bank statements to support the bank reconciliation statements for
verification and validation purposes and to record the P53,200.00 that may have
been overlooked in the process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor
to expedite the processing of documents for the titling of lands owned by the
Municipality. We further recommend that before any construction is made,
land ownership thru a certificate of title is obtained.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and Treasurer to discourage the disbursement of government
funds without complete documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund Officer to fully implement the planned
programs, activities and projects for disaster preparedness, response,
rehabilitation and recovery so that the MDRRM Fund would be utilized and
the intended benefits would be fully achieved.

We recommended that the Mayor direct the Solid Waste Management Unit
to develop an awareness program on the management of solid waste and
choose the appropriate means of communication in order for it to be
effective.

We recommended that a mechanism to measure the volume of waste and type
of waste should be placed at the site, which will provide baseline data to assist
in developing effective waste minimization strategies and future plans for
waste management.

9.5 We recommended that the Mayor direct the Solid Waste Management (SWM)
Focal Person to provide technical support for the waste segregation at the
barangay level.

9.6 We further recommended that the SWM Focal Person should implement a
color coding or appropriate means of identification to ensure that wastes are
properly segregated at the RCA.

We recommended that the Mayor direct the SWM Focal Person to maintain
disposal site records in the RCA.

We recommended that the RCA be fenced with a secured gate at the
entrance to minimize illegal visitors or scavengers on the area. This will also
help address the occupational health and safety issues by limiting access.

We recommended that the Mayor cause the allocation of funds to purchase
the appropriate safety clothing as prescribed in the Manual for Operation
and Maintenance at Sanitary Landfill; direct the SWM Focal Person to
ensure that any person entering the RCA must wear the proper safety
clothing so that health risks are minimized, and that the standards and
processes prescribed in the manual are complied with so that the operations
at the RCA are effective and safe.

We recommended that the MSWB convene regularly to address certain issues
in the formulation of the MSWM Plan and hasten its approval with the
NSWMC.

We recommended that the cover should be done on a daily basis. Waste in the
RCA should not be left open and strewn all over the place to protect health of
the community.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC Secretariat to
submit to the Audit Team all contracts with complete supporting documents
within five working days upon perfection thereof for review.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
and Treasurer to ensure that warranty securities posted for completed projects
in lieu of the 10% Retention Money is in accordance to Section of 62.2.3.3 of
RIRR of RA No. 9184 which is 30% of the contract cost, if in the form of
surety bond, 10% of contract cost if in the form of bank guarantee and 5% if
in cash.

1. make representation to the local legislative body to amend certain
provisions of the above-mentioned ordinance specially on Section 1.1
regarding the term of lease;
2. renew lease contracts for shorter periods pending demolition/utilization of
aid buildings/lots.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
monitor utilization of municipal government lots held for rent and impose
penalty in accordance with the penal provision of the Municipal Ordinance.

1. make representation to the local legislative body to amend certain
provisions of the Municipal Ordinance specifically Section 1.1 regarding
terms of lease;
2. renew lease contracts for shorter periods pending demolition of said
buildings;
3. direct the Municipal Treasurer to send demand letters citing the penal
provisions in Section 5 of the municipal ordinance that the non-payment of
rental due as provided in the ordinance is ground for the for the
cancellation of the contract of lease.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource
Management Officer to adhere to regulations on hiring of JOs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer
and Municipal Accountant to exert effort to enforce immediate settlement of
the audit disallowances. Likewise, ensure that all employees who are retiring
or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the disallowances and charges
for which they are liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular 2009-
006 that the settlement of disallowances and charges is made within the
prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to
establish a VAW Help Desk in their respective barangays staffed with
competent personnel who will ensure that violence against women cases are
addressed in a gender-responsive manner.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to
establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:

a) direct the General Services Office and the Accounting Office to exhaust all
effort in the determination of the particulars, whereabouts, and any documents
or information pertaining to the unreconciled/undetermined properties and
those properties captioned merely as beginning balances in lump-sum amount
under each PPE account. Make certain that these discrepancies would be
lessened as years pass by so that eventual elimination of these unidentified
properties could be achieved in the coming years, preferably sooner than later
S0 as to reflect fair balances of PPE accounts. If necessary, require the
submission from persons in charge of the PPE items of all the procedures
adopted by them in the reconciliation process in order to document the
process and move forward to the next possible course of actionis;

b. create an Inventory Committee (IC) who will conduct thorough physical
inventory taking of the municipal properties, preparation and submission of
reconciled RPCPPE with accounting records, so that existence, accuracy and
validity of all properties under the Property, Plant Equipment accounts
recorded in the books of accounts of the Municipality; and

c) require the Inventory Committee to submit to the Audit Team the
updates/development of the reconciliation made between the Accounting and
Property records every semester for verification and monitoring purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
effect the necessary reclassification to the Merchandise Inventory account from
Investment Property, Building account the cost of the two-door, two storey
apartment-style building of P1,195,617.01, and from Property, Plant and
Equipment-Land account the cost of the remaining 2,044 sq.mt lot in Barangay
Lipahan considering that the intention of the Municipality was to sell the
properties as per SB Resolution Nos. 108-2014; 107-2014 both dated October 13,
2014 and SB Resolution No. 028-2018 dated February 19, 2018, respectively, in
accordance with the provisions of PPSAS Nos. 12 and 16.

Likewise, we require the immediate submission of all documents supporting the
manner and basis by which the one unit of the two-door, two-storey apartment-
style building has been awarded to its current occupant, and proof of payments.
for the said unit, if any, for further analysis and evaluation of the Audit Team.
Housing projects should have a properly laid out guidelines in the grant and
award of housing units to ensure that the real intention of the project is protected
from biases and abuse of authority.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the immediate crafting of
guidelines for the scholarship program being implemented by his Office in order
to ensure that the recipients are really deserving of the financial assistance being
extended to elementary, high school, and college students. Said guidelines
should also include the procedures on how the financial assistance may be given
to students, either thru direct payment to students or a deduction of the amount
payable by each municipal scholar to their respective school so as to ensure that
said funds have served its purpose.

Likewise, require from the head of all recipient public schools to submit a
satisfactory explanations on why the receipt issued by them in receiving the funds
due the municipal scholars were not the prescribed official receipt printed by the
National Printing Office. Otherwise, require from the said school issuance of the
prescribed Official Receipts for government transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System
(GFPS) to ensure that the gender issues reported in the GPB and the GAD
Accomplishment Report are supported with sex-disaggregated data or relative
gender information. Said information is necessary to justify the issues cited as
gender related issues, where correct application of intervention thru various GAD
related activities is needed to address unequal opportunities for men and women.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

€) evaluate and secure relevant documents pertai g to all Financi nd Trust
Liabilities as well as Inter-Agency Payables, such as Accounts Payables, Due
from Officers and Employees, Guaranty/Security Deposit Payable, Due to
LGUs and Due to NGAs which are long outstanding and unsubstantiated for
proper disposition and de-recognition if warranted; and

d) make proper presentation in writing with the concerned government agencies
to confirm the existence of such obligation in their Office. If said agencies
have valid claims that match with the recorded obligation of the Municipality,
proceed to the processing of payment upon presentation of sufficient
documentation. Otherwise, revert these accounts to Unappropriated Surplus of
the General Fund after securing authorization of the Sangguniang Bayan, in
order to rid from the books any unsubstantiated claims, and to reflect a in the
financial statements a valid and fairly stated balances of liability accounts.

observations.recommendation:
Financial and Compliance

Receivables - Real Property Tax (RPT)/Special Education Tax (SET) and the corresponding Deferred Credits/Unearned Income - Deferred RPT/SET 
 
The balances of Real Property Tax (RPT) ) and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivables in the amounts of P84.087 million and P48.842 million, respectively, and the corresponding Deferred RPT/SET totaling P385.441 million as of December 31, 2017 remained doubtful due to the (a) non-correction/  adjustment of the beginning balances of the said accounts; (b) non-inclusion of RPUs with Unpaid RPT/SET for CY 2017; and (c) variances in the setting-up of tax receivables for the year which caused the understatement of the RPT Receivable/Deferred RPT and SET Receivable/Deferred SET by P19.027 million and P17.133 million, respectively.      

We recommended that the City Accounting Department coordinate with the City Treasury Department to look into the variances noted and/or account for the discrepancies noted in the RPT and SET Receivables, and make the necessary journal entry to correct the RPT/SET Receivables and the affected account balances in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction:
Fully
implemented | Management adheres to the recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
Tax Receivables and Deferred Credits account balances are unreliable due to the discrepancies in the records/reports generated from RPTAS which are not consistent with the Manual on Real Property Appraisal and Assessment Operations (MRPAAO), thus, resulted in the understatement of P2.134 million and P1.263 million for RPT and SET Receivables, respectively. Moreover, the breakdown of controls in the RPTAS and the absence of individual SLs for every real property tax category, cast doubts on the reliability of the generated records and reports from the RPTAS.

We recommended that:

The City Assessor:

Retain the records of the retired or cancelled PINs in the system to facilitate validation whether the RPUs are newly created/ discovered/consolidated;
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | The records of the retired or cancelled PINs are now stored in the History Data Entry in the RPTAS.

observations.recommendation: Refrain from removing existing permanent PINs/RPUs with outstanding collectibles in the records;
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | Management refrains from removing existing PINs/RPUs with outstanding collectibles.

observations.recommendation: See to it that every correction effected in the system is supported with approval from higher authority; and
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | Management adheres to the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: Immediately notify the CTD of any change/update of information regarding the RPUs to facilitate updating of its records and reports.
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | CAO regularly notifies the CTD of every change/update in the RPTAS.

Install additional system controls that would check duplicates to avoid occurrence of duplicate PINs/transactions being posted in the system;
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | Management adheres to the recommendation

observations.recommendation: Make sure that appropriate controls in the RPTAS are consistently in place and effective to ensure that system generated reports are accurate and complete; and
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | Appropriate controls are now in place.

observations.recommendation:
Inventories

The balances of Office Supplies Inventory (OSI) and Other Supplies and Materials Inventory (OSMI) accounts amounting to P9.794 million and P1.623 million, respectively, are doubtful due to (a) application of varying methods of recording the purchases and issuances of inventories; (b) inclusion in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) of the cost of items not taken up as Inventory; (c) unreconciled data between the issued Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS)  and the SSMI; (d) misclassification of inventory items in the books that resulted to the overstatement of the OSI account and the understatement of the  Office Supplies Expenses by P4.382 million; and the understatement of the OSMI account and overstatement of Other Supplies and Materials Expense (OSME) by P1.772 million; and  (e) improper charging of other general services amounting to P2.426 million to OSME. |  |  | 

We recommended that:

The CAD:

Reconcile the balances of the accounts Office Supplies Inventory, Other Supplies and Materials Inventory and their corresponding Expense accounts;
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | The CAD effected the necessary adjustments per JEV#100-2018-07-0214 and 100-2018-12-0377

observations.recommendation: Properly classify expenses according to nature and use to comply with the requirements of PPSAS 1.
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | Expenses are now properly classified.

observations.recommendation:
Rent Income-San Juan Arena

The terms of the contract for the lease of San Juan Arena Facility were not strictly followed, resulting to (a) delayed payments of rental fees; (b) over-collection of interest in the amount of P1.510 million; (c) non- issuance of postdated checks by the Operator to secure payment of rental fees; (d) non-submission of warranty security; (e) non-reimbursement by the Operator of the Arena of the insurance premium advanced by the City to the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS); (f) non-collection of fees for streamers, billboards or any form of advertisement; and (g) assignment of only one personnel at the Arena instead of nine personnel as per agreement. |  |  | 

We recommended that:

The CTD:

Compute correctly the interest for the delinquent payments of rental fees and properly monitor payments in accordance with the agreed schedule in the contract;
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | The CTD now monitors payment of these fees.

observations.recommendation: Conduct regular ocular inspection on the premises to account for the advertisements authorized by the Operator in the facility and collect the corresponding fees; and
observations.managementAction: Fully implemented | Inspection was made and payments were received for the advertisements.

total amount of P2,395,066.
